```mermaid
    subgraph PROCESSING[PROCESSING LOGIC]
        AVM[Compute Average OT]
        RT[Apply Rating to get BT]
        SUM[Sum all BTs to get NT]
        ALL[Add Allowances]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS[OUTPUTS]
        NT2[Normal Time]
        ST[Standard Time]
    end
    OT --> AVM
    AVM --> RT
    RF --> RT
    RT --> SUM
    SUM --> NT2
    NT2 --> ALL
    AL --> ALL
    ALL --> ST
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram — Allowances Breakdown

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Allowances A] --> B[Personal Allowance PA]
    A --> C[Fatigue Allowance FA]
    A --> D[Contingency Delay Allowance DA]
    B --> B1[Restroom Visits]
    B --> B2[Drinking Water]
    B --> B3[Personal Time]
    C --> C1[Constant FA: 4 percent]
    C --> C2[Variable FA: Posture, Lighting, Noise]
    C --> C3[Variable FA: Temperature and Humidity]
    D --> D1[Tool Breakage]
    D --> D2[Material Shortage]
    D --> D3[Power Interruptions]
    D --> D4[Instruction from Supervisor]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

### Question 1: Define Standard Time. List its main components.
**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Time** is the time allowed to a qualified worker, working at a normal pace (100% performance rating), to perform a specified job under standard conditions.

**Main Components (3 sub-parts for 1 mark each):**

1. **Observed Time (OT)**: Actual time recorded by stopwatch.
2. **Normal Time (NT)**: $NT = OT \times Rating Factor$. This is the time at standard performance.
3. **Allowances**: Time added for personal needs, fatigue recovery, and unavoidable delays. The total is given by:
$$
ST = NT \times (1 + A) \quad \text{or} \quad ST = \frac{NT}{1 - A}
$$

### Question 2: What is a Performance Rating? Why is it needed in Time Study?
**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Performance Rating** is a numerical factor (typically expressed as a percentage) that the timekeeper assigns to the worker's observed pace to convert the *observed time* into the *normal time*. The standard benchmark is **100%** (a normal, sustainable pace).

**Why It Is Needed (2 sub-points for 2 marks):**

1. **Compensates for worker variability**: No two workers operate at exactly the same pace. Rating normalizes the data to a common benchmark.
2. **Adjusts for non-standard effort**: If a worker was moving at 110% (faster than normal), the rating of 1.10 inflates the observed time to reflect what a *normal* worker would take. This ensures the standard is **fair and achievable**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module Internal Choice Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Model Solution**

A time study was carried out on a manual assembly operation consisting of **5 elements**. The operation was timed for **8 cycles** and the data is given below. The performance rating and the frequency of occurrence of each element are also given. Total allowance is **15% of standard time**. Calculate:
- (a) The Normal Time for the operation. **(7 Marks)**
- (b) The Standard Time per piece. **(7 Marks)**

| Element | OT₁ | OT₂ | OT₃ | OT₄ | OT₅ | OT₆ | OT₇ | OT₈ | Rating | Frequency per cycle |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.20 | 0.22 | 0.21 | 0.20 | 0.23 | 0.21 | 0.20 | 0.22 | 100% | 1 |
| 2 | 0.40 | 0.45 | 0.42 | 0.41 | 0.40 | 0.43 | 0.42 | 0.41 | 90% | 1 |
| 3 | 0.60 | 0.58 | 0.62 | 0.60 | 0.61 | 0.59 | 0.60 | 0.60 | 110% | 1 |
| 4 | 0.30 | 0.32 | 0.31 | 0.30 | 0.33 | 0.31 | 0.30 | 0.32 | 95% | 2 |
| 5 | 0.50 | 0.48 | 0.52 | 0.50 | 0.49 | 0.51 | 0.50 | 0.50 | 105% | 1 |

*(All times in minutes)*

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Calculation of Normal Time **(7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Calculate the Average Observed Time for each element:**

$$
\overline{OT_1} = \frac{0.20+0.22+0.21+0.20+0.23+0.21+0.20+0.22}{8} = \frac{1.69}{8} = 0.2113 \text{ min}
$$
$$
\overline{OT_2} = \frac{0.40+0.45+0.42+0.41+0.40+0.43+0.42+0.41}{8} = \frac{3.34}{8} = 0.4175 \text{ min}
$$
$$
\overline{OT_3} = \frac{0.60+0.58+0.62+0.60+0.61+0.59+0.60+0.60}{8} = \frac{4.80}{8} = 0.6000 \text{ min}
$$
$$
\overline{OT_4} = \frac{0.30+0.32+0.31+0.30+0.33+0.31+0.30+0.32}{8} = \frac{2.49}{8} = 0.3113 \text{ min}
$$
$$
\overline{OT_5} = \frac{0.50+0.48+0.52+0.50+0.49+0.51+0.50+0.50}{8} = \frac{4.00}{8} = 0.5000 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2: Multiply by frequency to get effective average time per cycle:**

$$
E_1 = 0.2113 \times 1 = 0.2113 \text{ min}
$$
$$
E_2 = 0.4175 \times 1 = 0.4175 \text{ min}
$$
$$
E_3 = 0.6000 \times 1 = 0.6000 \text{ min}
$$
$$
E_4 = 0.3113 \times 2 = 0.6226 \text{ min}
$$
$$
E_5 = 0.5000 \times 1 = 0.5000 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Performance Rating to get Basic Time (1 mark per row, 2 marks for sum):**

$$
BT_1 = 0.2113 \times 1.00 = 0.2113 \text{ min}
$$
$$
BT_2 = 0.4175 \times 0.90 = 0.3758 \text{ min}
$$
$$
BT_3 = 0.6000 \times 1.10 = 0.6600 \text{ min}
$$
$$
BT_4 = 0.6226 \times 0.95 = 0.5915 \text{ min}
$$
$$
BT_5 = 0.5000 \times 1.05 = 0.5250 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4: Sum all Basic Times to get Normal Time (1 mark):**

$$
NT = 0.2113 + 0.3758 + 0.6600 + 0.5915 + 0.5250
$$
$$
\boxed{NT = 2.3636 \text{ min}}
$$

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Calculation of Standard Time **(7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Identify the correct formula based on problem statement (2 marks):**

> Since allowance is given as 15% of **Standard Time**, the correct formula is:
$$
ST = \frac{NT}{1 - A} = \frac{2.3636}{1 - 0.15} = \frac{2.3636}{0.85}
$$
$$
\boxed{ST = 2.7807 \text{ min per piece}}
$$

**Step 2: Verification and Step-by-Step Logical Presentation (3 marks):**

The working is presented in a structured table:

| Element | Avg OT | Freq | Effective OT | Rating | Basic Time (min) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.2113 | 1 | 0.2113 | 1.00 | 0.2113 |
| 2 | 0.4175 | 1 | 0.4175 | 0.90 | 0.3758 |
| 3 | 0.6000 | 1 | 0.6000 | 1.10 | 0.6600 |
| 4 | 0.3113 | 2 | 0.6226 | 0.95 | 0.5915 |
| 5 | 0.5000 | 1 | 0.5000 | 1.05 | 0.5250 |
| **Total** | — | — | — | — | **2.3636** |

- **Normal Time (NT)** = 2.3636 min
- **Allowance (15% of ST)** = 0.15 × 2.7807 = 0.4171 min
- **Standard Time (ST)** = NT + Allowance = 2.3636 + 0.4171 = **2.7807 min** ✓

**Step 3: Production Planning Interpretation (2 marks):**

If the factory works 8 hours per day with one worker, the daily production capacity is:
$$
\text{Capacity} = \frac{8 \times 60}{2.7807} = \frac{480}{2.7807} \approx 172.6 \text{ pieces per day}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

A time study was conducted on a turning operation. The job is divided into **3 elements** and observed for **5 cycles**. The total normal time for the operation is computed as **4.20 minutes**. The worker is provided with the following allowances:
- Personal Allowance: 7%
- Fatigue Allowance: 10%
- Contingency Delay Allowance: 3%
- **(a)** Classify the allowances and explain each in 2-3 lines. **(7 Marks)**
- **(b)** Calculate the standard time for the operation if the allowances are based on **(i)** Normal Time, and **(ii)** Standard Time. **(7 Marks)**

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Classification of Allowances **(7 Marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Allowance Classification (2 marks for naming, 5 marks for explanation)**

Allowances are broadly classified into **three categories**:

1. **Personal Allowance (PA) = 7%**: This is the time allowed to a worker for attending to personal needs such as restroom visits, drinking water, washing hands, and other biological necessities. It is generally fixed at **5-7%** of basic time and does not depend on working conditions. **(2 marks)**

2. **Fatigue Allowance (FA) = 10%**: This is the time given to a worker to recover from physical and mental fatigue caused by the work. It includes a **constant component (4%)** for general fatigue and a **variable component** that depends on posture (standing, sitting), lighting, noise, temperature, humidity, and the strenuousness of the work. A value of 10% is considered moderate for standing work. **(2.5 marks)**

3. **Contingency/Delay Allowance (DA) = 3%**: This covers unavoidable and unpredictable delays such as minor tool breakage, waiting for materials, brief power interruptions, and short consultations with the supervisor. It is the hardest to estimate accurately and is usually based on past experience or company records. **(2.5 marks)**

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Standard Time Calculation **(7 Marks)**

**Total Allowance Factor:**
$$
A_{total} = 7\% + 10\% + 3\% = 20\% = 0.20
$$

**Case (i): Allowance is 20% of Normal Time (3.5 marks):**

Using the formula:
$$
ST = NT \times (1 + A) = 4.20 \times (1 + 0.20)
$$
$$
\boxed{ST_i = 4.20 \times 1.20 = 5.04 \text{ min}}
$$

**Case (ii): Allowance is 20% of Standard Time (3.5 marks):**

Using the formula:
$$
ST = \frac{NT}{1 - A} = \frac{4.20}{1 - 0.20} = \frac{4.20}{0.80}
$$
$$
\boxed{ST_{ii} = 5.25 \text{ min}}
$$

**Comparison (bonus conceptual point):**
$$
\Delta ST = 5.25 - 5.04 = 0.21 \text{ min}
$$
The standard time is higher in Case (ii) because a larger allowance is recovered ($0.84$ min vs. $0.84$ min) — interestingly, both yield different effective allowances. The Case (ii) interpretation is preferred by ILG and ILO as it represents a **circular reference** (the allowance time itself is part of the standard time being computed).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost KTU Students 2-4 Marks Each**

1. **Confusing "Allowance on NT" vs "Allowance on ST"**: This is the **#1 mistake**. Read carefully whether the problem says "15% of Normal Time" or "15% of Standard Time". The formulas are different.

2. **Forgetting to multiply by Frequency Factor**: If an element occurs twice per cycle (e.g., tightening two bolts), its effective time is $2 \times$ the element's time. Missing this loses full marks on that element.

3. **Not computing the Average OT separately for each element**: Some students compute a single average across all elements — this is **fundamentally wrong**. Average must be computed *per element* across cycles.

4. **Mixing up the Rating Application**: The rating multiplies the **Average Observed Time**, not the sum. The correct sequence is: Average → Multiply by Rating → Sum across elements.

5. **Forgetting Units**: Always mention **minutes or seconds** in the final answer. Even if the value is correct, missing units can lead to 0.5 mark deduction.

6. **No Verification Step**: Top KTU scorers add a final verification: $NT + Allowance = ST$ (Case i) or $ST - NT = Allowance$ (Case ii). This shows the examiner you understand the concept deeply.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read This Before the Exam**

- ☐ **Time Study** is a work measurement technique used to determine **Standard Time**.
- ☐ The **Standard Performance** is defined as a **walking pace of 3 mph on level ground** (100% rating).
- ☐ The **first step** is method study, not timing. Time an inefficient method is meaningless.
- ☐ The **worker must be qualified**, trained, and cooperative. Studying an apprentice is invalid.
- ☐ The job must be broken into **small elements** (typically 0.5 to 2 minutes long).
- ☐ **Performance Rating** is a **subjective judgment** by the timekeeper — it is the biggest source of variability in time study.
- ☐ Three formulas to remember:
  1. $NT = OT \times R$
  2. $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ — when $A$ is a fraction of **NT**
  3. $ST = \dfrac{NT}{1 - A}$ — when $A$ is a fraction of **ST**
- ☐ **Allowances** = Personal (5-7%) + Fatigue (4% base + variable) + Contingency (5-10%) = typically **15-20%**.
- ☐ **Frequency Factor** multiplies the element's basic time when the element occurs multiple times per cycle.
- ☐ **Standard Time is the foundation** for production planning, costing, wage incentives, and line balancing.
- ☐ For a 14-mark problem, always present your answer in a **structured table** — this earns method marks.
- ☐ End your answer with an **interpretation** (e.g., "Hence, the standard time is X minutes, meaning a worker can produce Y pieces per 8-hour shift"). This is a high-value add-on for full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work sampling on recorded tasks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL408 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Work sampling on recorded tasks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Work Sampling** is a statistical technique of determining the proportion of time spent by a worker (or machine) on various activities by taking a large number of **random, instantaneous observations** and recording the activity occurring at that instant. When applied to **recorded tasks** (e.g., pre-recorded video, audio logs, or timestamped operational logs), the same statistical principle is used, but the observer studies a **pre-existing recording** of the work rather than live activity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Work Sampling):**
> "A statistical method of determining the percentage of time spent by a worker on various activities by taking a large number of random, instantaneous observations."

The recorded-task variant differs only in the **data acquisition medium** — the analytical/statistical foundation remains identical.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you want to find out how a child spends a typical school day — studying, playing, eating, sleeping. Instead of shadowing the child for 8 hours straight, you walk into the classroom **at random moments** (say, 400 times over a week), glance at what the child is doing, and tick a box. After hundreds of such random "snapshots," the percentage of ticks in each box approximates the true percentage of the day spent on that activity.

**Work sampling = taking random snapshots to estimate time proportions.**

In the **recorded-task** version, the child is wearing a head-camera all day. You (the observer) sit at a computer, scrub through the video, and at **random timestamps** you pause and note the activity. The advantage: you can rewind, replay, and the recording is **non-destructively observable**, removing the Hawthorne effect (workers behaving differently when watched live).

## 1.3 Key Terms & Standard Metrics

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Random observation** | An instantaneous reading taken at an unpredictable instant |
| **Activity category** | A mutually exclusive class of work (e.g., productive, idle, setup) |
| **Proportion (p)** | Fraction of observations falling in a category |
| **Confidence level (1−α)** | Probability that the true proportion lies in the interval, usually **95%** |
| **Absolute accuracy (A)** | Acceptable error margin in the proportion estimate (e.g., ±0.04) |
| **Sample size (N)** | Total number of random observations required |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "recorded" tasks?** Recording eliminates observer fatigue bias, allows **multiple observers** to study the same task, and the recording can be **archived** for re-analysis. It is the modern, industry-preferred form of work sampling.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Statistical Foundation — The Binomial Basis

Every random observation is essentially a **Bernoulli trial**: the worker is either performing activity *X* (success, with true probability *p*) or not (failure, with probability *1−p*).

Across *N* independent random observations, the number of times activity *X* is observed (*X_obs*) follows a **Binomial distribution**:

$$
X_{obs} \sim \text{Binomial}(N, p)
$$

The **sample proportion** is:

$$
\hat{p} = \frac{X_{obs}}{N}
$$

For large *N*, by the Central Limit Theorem, $\hat{p}$ is approximately **normally distributed**:

$$
\hat{p} \sim N\!\left(p,\ \frac{p(1-p)}{N}\right)
$$

## 2.2 The Three Core Formulas (KTU Must-Know)

### Formula 1 — Sample Size for Given Absolute Accuracy

$$
N = \frac{Z^{2}\, p\,(1-p)}{A^{2}}
$$

where:
- *Z* = standard normal deviate (1.96 for 95% confidence, 2.58 for 99%)
- *p* = estimated proportion of time for the activity
- *1−p* = complement
- *A* = desired absolute accuracy (e.g., 0.04 means ±4%)

### Formula 2 — Confidence Interval for the True Proportion

$$
p = \hat{p} \pm Z \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{N}}
$$

This is the interval within which the true proportion *p* lies with the chosen confidence.

### Formula 3 — Standard Time from Work Sampling

$$
\text{Standard Time} = \text{Normal Time} \times (1 + \text{Allowance})
$$

$$
\text{Normal Time} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} (p_{i} \times \text{Average Time}_{i})
$$

where *k* is the number of mutually exclusive activity categories.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU question on work sampling uses one of these.**

| # | Formula | Purpose | Standard Value Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{Z^{2}\, p\,(1-p)}{A^{2}}$ | Required sample size | $Z = 1.96$ (95%) |
| 2 | $p = \hat{p} \pm Z\sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{N}}$ | Confidence interval | $\hat{p}$ = observed proportion |
| 3 | $A = 2 Z \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{N}}$ | Achieved accuracy | Computed after sampling |
| 4 | $\text{Normal Time} = \sum p_{i} \cdot t_{i}$ | Standard time calc. | $t_i$ = avg. time of activity *i* |
| 5 | $\text{Std. Time} = \text{NT} \times (1 + \text{Allowance})$ | Final allowance-included time | Allowance usually 10–25% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Work sampling on recorded tasks is the **backbone of time-and-motion studies in modern manufacturing, healthcare (nurse activity profiling), call centers, and software engineering** (developer activity logs). It allows ISO-compliant time standards without disrupting the workflow.

## 2.4 Procedure Logic — Why Each Step Exists

1. **Define activity categories** → ensures *mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive* (MECE) classification, eliminating double-counting.
2. **Estimate pilot proportion (*p*)** → required because the formula needs an initial guess (use 0.5 if completely unknown — this gives the most conservative, largest sample size).
3. **Decide confidence level and accuracy** → 95% / ±0.04 are KTU default values.
4. **Compute sample size *N*** → guarantees statistical validity.
5. **Generate random timestamps** → ensures independence of observations.
6. **Observe the recording at each timestamp** → the actual "snapshot."
7. **Compute $\hat{p}$ for each activity** → the empirical proportion.
8. **Verify accuracy achieved** → if $A_{achieved} > A_{desired}$, take more observations.
9. **Compute standard time** → the engineering deliverable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sample-Size Formula

We start from the **confidence interval** requirement: the half-width of the confidence interval must not exceed the desired accuracy *A*.

**Step 1 — Write the half-width of the confidence interval**

The half-width (margin of error) of the proportion estimate is:

$$
E = Z \cdot \sigma_{\hat{p}}
$$

where $\sigma_{\hat{p}}$ is the standard error of the sample proportion.

**Step 2 — Express the standard error of a proportion**

For a binomial proportion, the standard deviation is $\sqrt{p(1-p)/N}$. Therefore:

$$
\sigma_{\hat{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{N}}
$$

**Step 3 — Set the half-width equal to the desired accuracy**

$$
A = Z \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{N}}
$$

**Step 4 — Square both sides to remove the square root**

$$
A^{2} = Z^{2} \cdot \frac{p(1-p)}{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for *N* algebraically**

$$
N = \frac{Z^{2} \cdot p(1-p)}{A^{2}}
$$

This is the **fundamental work-sampling sample-size formula** used in every KTU numerical.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem (Typical KTU ESE):**
A preliminary study shows a machine is idle approximately 30% of the time. How many random observations are needed to estimate the idle proportion within **±4% accuracy at 95% confidence**?

**Given:**
- $p = 0.30$ (idle proportion estimate)
- $1 - p = 0.70$
- $A = 0.04$
- $Z = 1.96$ (95% confidence, two-tailed)

**Step 1 — Substitute into the formula**

$$
N = \frac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{A^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Plug numerical values**

$$
N = \frac{(1.96)^{2} \cdot (0.30) \cdot (0.70)}{(0.04)^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator**

$$
(1.96)^{2} = 3.8416
$$

$$
0.30 \times 0.70 = 0.21
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 3.8416 \times 0.21 = 0.8067
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator**

$$
(0.04)^{2} = 0.0016
$$

**Step 5 — Divide**

$$
N = \frac{0.8067}{0.0016} = 504.19
$$

**Step 6 — Round up to next whole number (always round up for sample size)**

$$
N = 505 \text{ observations}
$$

**Conclusion:** A minimum of **505 random observations** must be taken to achieve the desired accuracy.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing *p* When Unknown

| Situation | Recommended *p* | Resulting *N* | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| No prior information | **0.50** | Maximum (most conservative) | $p(1-p)$ peaks at 0.25 |
| Idle time suspected to be high | 0.40 – 0.50 | Large | Conservative |
| Machine known to be productive most of the time | 0.10 – 0.20 | Smaller | $p(1-p)$ is small |
| Activity known to dominate (e.g., 80%) | 0.80 | Moderate | $0.8 \times 0.2 = 0.16$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick:** When *p* is unknown, examiners expect you to use **p = 0.5**. This guarantees the maximum possible sample size and the most statistically safe estimate.

## 3.4 Comprehensive Numerical — Standard Time from Work Sampling

**Problem:**
In a work-sampling study on a recorded assembly task, **600 random observations** were taken. The results classified into three categories:

| Activity | Observations | Avg. Time per Occurrence (min) |
|---|---|---|
| Productive work | 420 | 1.20 |
| Idle (waiting) | 120 | 0.80 |
| Re-work/reject | 60 | 2.50 |

The **personal, fatigue, and delay allowance** is **15%**. Compute the standard time per cycle.

**Step 1 — Compute sample proportions**

$$
\hat{p}_{\text{prod}} = \frac{420}{600} = 0.70
$$

$$
\hat{p}_{\text{idle}} = \frac{120}{600} = 0.20
$$

$$
\hat{p}_{\text{rework}} = \frac{60}{600} = 0.10
$$

**Step 2 — Verify proportions sum to 1**

$$
0.70 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Normal Time per cycle**

$$
\text{Normal Time} = \sum p_{i} \cdot t_{i}
$$

$$
= (0.70 \times 1.20) + (0.20 \times 0.80) + (0.10 \times 2.50)
$$

$$
= 0.840 + 0.160 + 0.250 = 1.250 \text{ min/cycle}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the allowance to get Standard Time**

$$
\text{Standard Time} = 1.250 \times (1 + 0.15)
$$

$$
= 1.250 \times 1.15 = 1.4375 \text{ min/cycle}
$$

**Step 5 — Optional: Verify achieved accuracy at 95% confidence for the rework category**

$$
A = 2 \times 1.96 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.10 \times 0.90}{600}}
$$

$$
= 3.92 \times \sqrt{0.00015} = 3.92 \times 0.01225 \approx 0.048
$$

So the achieved accuracy is **±0.048** (≈ ±4.8%) — slightly coarser than ±4%, acceptable for the rework category given its low proportion.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Work Sampling Procedure on Recorded Tasks

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Study Objective] --> B[Identify Activity Categories MECE]
    B --> C[Conduct Pilot Study or Use Prior Data]
    C --> D[Estimate Initial Proportion p]
    D --> E[Choose Confidence Level 1-alpha and Accuracy A]
    E --> F[Compute Required Sample Size N]
    F --> G[Generate N Random Timestamps]
    G --> H[Load Recorded Video/Log File]
    H --> I[For Each Random Timestamp: Pause and Record Activity]
    I --> J[Count Observations per Category]
    J --> K[Compute Sample Proportion phat]
    K --> L{Achieved Accuracy A within Target?}
    L -- No --> M[Add More Random Observations]
    M --> I
    L -- Yes --> N[Compute 95% Confidence Interval]
    N --> O[Calculate Normal Time and Standard Time]
    O --> P[Document and Report Standard Time]
    P --> Q[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Work-Sampling Software Tool

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Layer]
        R[Recorded Video File]
        L[Timestamp Log CSV]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer[Processing Engine]
        RNG[Random Timestamp Generator]
        OBS[Observation Module]
        CLASS[Activity Classifier]
    end

    subgraph AnalyticsLayer[Statistical Engine]
        SS[Sample Size Calculator]
        PROP[Proportion Estimator]
        CI[Confidence Interval Module]
        ST[Standard Time Module]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[Output Layer]
        TAB[Tabulated Results]
        REP[Final Report PDF]
    end

    R --> OBS
    L --> RNG
    RNG --> OBS
    OBS --> CLASS
    CLASS --> PROP
    SS --> RNG
    PROP --> CI
    PROP --> ST
    CI --> TAB
    ST --> TAB
    TAB --> REP
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Randomness & Statistical Validity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RND[Randomness of Observations] --> IND[Independence of Trials]
    IND --> BIN[Binomial Distribution Applies]
    BIN --> CLT[Central Limit Theorem for Large N]
    CLT --> NORM[Normal Approximation of phat]
    NORM --> CI[Confidence Interval Calculation]
    CI --> DEC[Engineering Decision: Standard Time]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a stage in the experimental or analytical flow. Arrows indicate data/information transfer. The loop in the procedure flowchart (steps L → M → I) is the **accuracy-convergence loop** — additional observations are taken until the desired accuracy is achieved.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define work sampling. List any two advantages of conducting work sampling on recorded tasks as compared to live observation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Work sampling** is a statistical technique used to determine the proportion of time spent by workers/machines on various activities by taking a large number of **random, instantaneous observations**.

**Two advantages of using recorded tasks:**
1. **Eliminates the Hawthorne effect** — workers are not aware of being observed in real time, so their natural behavior is captured.
2. **Allows repeated and parallel analysis** — the same recording can be re-analyzed by multiple observers, improving reliability and enabling operator-paced review without time pressure.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for two advantages)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is the value of *p* = 0.5 used in the sample-size formula when no prior estimate of the activity proportion is available?**

**Model Answer:**

The sample-size formula is:

$$
N = \frac{Z^{2}\, p\,(1-p)}{A^{2}}
$$

The product $p(1-p)$ is a **quadratic function of *p*** that reaches its **maximum value of 0.25 at p = 0.5**. Using *p* = 0.5 therefore yields the **largest (most conservative) sample size**, guaranteeing that the desired accuracy is met regardless of the true proportion. This makes it the **safe default** when no prior information is available.

*(3 marks: 1 mark identifying max of p(1−p) + 1 mark formula + 1 mark concluding statement)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** A work-sampling study is to be conducted on a recorded manufacturing operation. Preliminary observations suggest the machine is **idle 35% of the time**. Calculate the **number of random observations** required to estimate the idle-time proportion within **±3% accuracy at 99% confidence**. (7 marks)

**(b)** After conducting the study with the calculated sample size, **180 observations** of idle time were recorded out of the total sample. Construct the **99% confidence interval** for the true idle-time proportion and **interpret** it. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Given:**
- $p = 0.35$ (estimated idle proportion)
- $1 - p = 0.65$
- $A = 0.03$ (desired accuracy)
- $Z = 2.58$ (99% confidence, two-tailed)

**Step 1 — Apply the sample-size formula**

$$
N = \frac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{A^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$
N = \frac{(2.58)^{2} \cdot (0.35) \cdot (0.65)}{(0.03)^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator**

$$
(2.58)^{2} = 6.6564
$$

$$
0.35 \times 0.65 = 0.2275
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 6.6564 \times 0.2275 = 1.5143
$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator**

$$
(0.03)^{2} = 0.0009
$$

**Step 5 — Divide and round up**

$$
N = \frac{1.5143}{0.0009} = 1682.6 \rightarrow N = 1683 \text{ observations}
$$

**Final Answer (a):** **1683 random observations** are required.

*(7 marks: 1 mark data + 2 marks formula + 3 marks computation + 1 mark rounding)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given (post-study):**
- Total observations $N = 1683$
- Idle observations $X_{obs} = 180$
- $Z = 2.58$ (99% confidence)

**Step 1 — Compute sample proportion**

$$
\hat{p} = \frac{180}{1683} = 0.1069
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the standard error**

$$
SE = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{N}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1069 \times 0.8931}{1683}}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{\frac{0.0955}{1683}} = \sqrt{5.674 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.007533
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the margin of error**

$$
E = Z \cdot SE = 2.58 \times 0.007533 = 0.01943
$$

**Step 4 — Construct the confidence interval**

$$
p = \hat{p} \pm E = 0.1069 \pm 0.0194
$$

$$
\Rightarrow p \in [0.0875,\ 0.1263]
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret**

We are **99% confident** that the true idle-time proportion of the machine lies between **8.75% and 12.63%**. This interval is well within the originally desired ±3% accuracy (±0.03), so the study objective is satisfied.

*(7 marks: 1 mark proportion + 2 marks SE & ME + 2 marks CI + 2 marks interpretation)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure** for conducting work sampling on a recorded video task. (7 marks)

**(b)** During a work-sampling study, the following data were obtained:

| Activity | Observed Count | Avg. Time per Occurrence (sec) |
|---|---|---|
| Assembling | 450 | 25 |
| Material handling | 300 | 18 |
| Inspection | 150 | 12 |
| Idle | 100 | — |

Total observations = 1000. Allowance = **20%**. Compute the **Standard Time per cycle** and the **achieved accuracy** for the idle category at 95% confidence. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Define the objective** of the study (e.g., determine proportion of productive vs. idle time).

**Step 2 — Identify and define activity categories** (MECE — Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive). E.g., Assembling, Material handling, Inspection, Idle.

**Step 3 — Conduct a pilot study** (or use historical data) to estimate the initial proportion *p* for each category.

**Step 4 — Select confidence level and accuracy** (typical: 95% confidence, ±4% accuracy).

**Step 5 — Compute the required sample size** using $N = \dfrac{Z^{2}\, p\,(1-p)}{A^{2}}$.

**Step 6 — Generate *N* random timestamps** using a random-number generator (e.g., Excel `RAND()` function or a random-number table).

**Step 7 — Load the recorded video** and observe the worker's activity at each random timestamp. Pause the video, classify the activity, and record the tally.

**Step 8 — After all observations, compute** the sample proportion $\hat{p}$ for each activity.

**Step 9 — Verify achieved accuracy** using $A_{achieved} = 2Z \sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/N}$. If not within target, take more observations and repeat from Step 7.

**Step 10 — Construct confidence intervals** and **compute the Standard Time** using $\text{ST} = \text{Normal Time} \times (1 + \text{Allowance})$.

**Step 11 — Document results** in a formal report with statistical confidence statements.

*(7 marks: 1 mark for objective + 2 marks for steps 1–5 + 2 marks for steps 6–9 + 2 marks for steps 10–11)*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Compute sample proportions**

Total observations $N = 1000$

$$
\hat{p}_{\text{asm}} = 0.450,\quad \hat{p}_{\text{han}} = 0.300,\quad \hat{p}_{\text{ins}} = 0.150,\quad \hat{p}_{\text{idle}} = 0.100
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Normal Time**

$$
\text{Normal Time} = \sum p_{i} \cdot t_{i}
$$

$$
= (0.450 \times 25) + (0.300 \times 18) + (0.150 \times 12) + (0.100 \times 0)
$$

$$
= 11.250 + 5.400 + 1.800 + 0 = 18.450 \text{ sec/cycle}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply allowance to get Standard Time**

$$
\text{Standard Time} = 18.450 \times (1 + 0.20) = 18.450 \times 1.20 = 22.14 \text{ sec/cycle}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute achieved accuracy for idle category at 95% confidence**

$Z = 1.96$, $\hat{p}_{\text{idle}} = 0.10$, $N = 1000$

$$
A_{\text{achieved}} = 2 \times 1.96 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.10 \times 0.90}{1000}}
$$

$$
= 3.92 \times \sqrt{0.00009} = 3.92 \times 0.009487 = 0.0372
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret accuracy**

The achieved accuracy for the idle category is **±0.0372 or ±3.72%**, which is well within the typical ±4% engineering standard.

**Final Answer (b):** **Standard Time = 22.14 sec/cycle**, **Achieved Accuracy for idle = ±3.72%**.

*(7 marks: 1 mark proportions + 2 marks Normal Time + 2 marks Standard Time + 2 marks accuracy)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting to round *N* UP** — sample size must always be rounded **up** to the next integer. Using $\lfloor N \rfloor$ instead of $\lceil N \rceil$ loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong *Z* value** — at **95% use Z = 1.96**; at **99% use Z = 2.58**; at **90% use Z = 1.645**. Mixing these up is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Using $\hat{p}$ in the sample-size formula** — the formula uses the **estimated/expected *p*** (pre-study), **not** the post-study $\hat{p}$. The post-study $\hat{p}$ is used only in the **confidence interval** formula.
> 4. **Forgetting the idle time = 0** — in standard-time computation, idle categories contribute 0 to Normal Time (no work is done), but their proportion still affects confidence calculations.
> 5. **Not stating the assumption** — always mention that observations are **independent and random**; this is a 1-mark often missed.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — go through this list before entering the exam hall:**

- ✅ Work sampling = **random instantaneous observations** to estimate **proportions of time**.
- ✅ Recorded-task version = applies the same statistics to **video/audio logs**.
- ✅ Core formula: $N = \dfrac{Z^{2}\, p\,(1-p)}{A^{2}}$.
- ✅ Always use **p = 0.5** if no prior estimate exists (gives maximum N).
- ✅ 95% confidence → **Z = 1.96**; 99% confidence → **Z = 2.58**.
- ✅ Always **round up** the computed sample size.
- ✅ Confidence interval: $p = \hat{p} \pm Z\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/N}$.
- ✅ Achieved accuracy: $A = 2Z\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/N}$.
- ✅ Normal Time $= \sum p_i \cdot t_i$.
- ✅ Standard Time $=$ Normal Time $\times (1 + \text{Allowance})$.
- ✅ Idle activities contribute **zero to Normal Time** but **count toward proportions**.
- ✅ Activity categories must be **MECE** (Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive).
- ✅ Recorded tasks remove the **Hawthorne effect** and allow **re-analysis**.
- ✅ Number of observations needed is **inversely proportional to A²** — halving the accuracy requires 4× more observations.
- ✅ The **Central Limit Theorem** justifies the normal approximation for large *N* (typically *N* > 30).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of standard time with methods time measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL408 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of standard time with methods time measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Methods Time Measurement (MTM)** is a **predetermined motion time system (PMTS)** developed by **H.C. Maynard, G.J. Stegemerten, and J.L. Schwab** in **1948**. It is a work measurement technique used to determine the **standard time** required to perform a given task by breaking it down into its **basic human motions (therbligs)**, assigning **predetermined time values (TMUs)** to each motion from a standardized data table, and summing them to obtain the **normal time** for the task.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, MTM is a **synthetic work measurement method** that synthesizes a job's standard time without physically timing an operator, making it purely an **analytical (synthetic) estimation technique** based on fundamental human motion data.

### 1.2 The Fundamental Unit: TMU

The **Time Measurement Unit (TMU)** is the bedrock of the MTM system.

$$1 \text{ TMU} = 0.00001 \text{ hour} = 0.0006 \text{ minute} = 0.036 \text{ second}$$

$$\therefore 1 \text{ second} = 27.78 \text{ TMU} \approx 28 \text{ TMU}$$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe for Motion" 🍳

Imagine you want to know exactly how long it takes to make a cup of tea, but you don't have a stopwatch. Instead, you have a **recipe book** that says:
- *Reach for the kettle* → takes **10 seconds**
- *Move the kettle to the stove* → takes **15 seconds**
- *Turn the knob* → takes **5 seconds**
- *Pour water into the cup* → takes **12 seconds**

By **adding up the pre-fixed values from the book**, you estimate the total time = **42 seconds**. 

**MTM works exactly like this recipe book for industrial motions!**
- The **recipe book** = the **MTM data table**
- Each **cooking step** = a **basic human motion (therblig)**
- The **time for each step** = the **TMU value**
- The **total time of the recipe** = the **normal time** of the task

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** MTM is a *synthetic* method — it **does not require stopwatch observation**. All times are pre-determined from extensive laboratory research on **average qualified operators** working under **normal working conditions**.

### 1.4 Standard Time — The Final Objective

The ultimate output of any MTM study is the **Standard Time (ST)** of the operation:

$$\text{Standard Time (ST)} = \text{Normal Time (NT)} + \text{Allowances (A)}$$

Where:
$$\text{Normal Time (NT)} = \text{Sum of TMU values for all motions} \times \text{Rating Factor}$$

> **Allowance** = the extra time added to compensate for **fatigue, personal needs, contingencies, and unavoidable delays**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Basic Human Motions Recognized by MTM

MTM recognizes **eight (8) fundamental motion categories**. Each motion category has multiple sub-cases depending on the **distance**, **type of object**, and **complexity** of the action.

> [!NOTE]
> The following table forms the **MTM Master Reference** that examiners love to test.

| # | Motion Category | Symbol | Description | Distance/Case Dependence |
|---|-----------------|--------|-------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Reach** | **R** | Empty-hand movement to an object | Depends on **distance (A, B, C, D, E)** |
| 2 | **Move** | **M** | Hand movement carrying an object | Depends on **distance and object size** |
| 3 | **Turn** | **T** | Rotational motion of forearm/wrist | Depends on **degrees of rotation** |
| 4 | **Grasp** | **G** | Closing fingers around an object | Depends on **size, shape, location** |
| 5 | **Position** | **P** | Aligning, orienting, or engaging an object | Depends on **symmetry, difficulty** |
| 6 | **Release** | **RL** | Letting go of an object | Generally constant (~ **2 TMU**) |
| 7 | **Disengage** | **D** | Breaking contact between objects | Depends on **degree of fit (Loose, Light, Medium, Tight)** |
| 8 | **Apply Pressure** | **AP** | Overcoming resistance (pressing) | Generally constant (~ **10.6 TMU**) |

Additional auxiliary motions include **Eye Travel (ET)**, **Eye Focus (EF)**, **Crank Motion (C)**, **Foot Motion (FM)**, **Leg Motion (LM)**, and **Bend & Arise (B/AB)**.

### 2.2 The MTM Distance Cases (Reach & Move)

The **Reach (R)** and **Move (M)** motion times vary with the distance traveled, classified into five cases:

| Case | Distance Range | Typical TMU |
|------|----------------|-------------|
| **Case A** | Up to 2 inches (~5 cm) | ~ **2 TMU** |
| **Case B** | Over 2 up to 12 inches (~5 to 30 cm) | ~ **7 – 10 TMU** |
| **Case C** | Over 12 up to 16 inches (~30 to 40 cm) | ~ **11 – 12 TMU** |
| **Case D** | Over 16 up to 32 inches (~40 to 80 cm) | ~ **13 – 16 TMU** |
| **Case E** | Over 32 inches (> 80 cm) | ~ **17 – 20+ TMU** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** When a Move motion involves placing an object into a position requiring alignment (e.g., inserting a peg into a hole), the MTM data table provides a **combined "Move + Position" (MP)** value to avoid separate analysis.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | TMU to Seconds | $1 \text{ TMU} = 0.036 \text{ s}$ | seconds | Convert single TMU value |
| 2 | TMU to Minutes | $1 \text{ TMU} = 0.0006 \text{ min}$ | minutes | Convert summed TMU |
| 3 | TMU to Hours | $1 \text{ TMU} = 0.00001 \text{ h}$ | hours | Convert to hours for standard time |
| 4 | Normal Time | $NT = \left(\sum TMU_{i}\right) \times R$ | minutes | Apply performance rating |
| 5 | Standard Time | $ST = NT + A = NT \times \left(1 + \dfrac{A\%}{100}\right)$ | minutes | Add allowances |
| 6 | Allowance Factor | $A = NT \times \dfrac{A\%}{100}$ | minutes | Compute allowance value |
| 7 | Performance Rating | $R = \dfrac{\text{Observed Speed}}{\text{Normal Speed}}$ | dimensionless | Adjustment factor |
| 8 | Number of Cycles | $N = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{ST \text{ per cycle}}$ | cycles | Capacity planning |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** Rating Factor (R) is often taken as **1.0** (100%) in MTM because the TMU values are *already* based on a **normal-qualified operator**. Applying R = 1 is the default unless explicitly stated otherwise.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

MTM is widely used in:
- 🏭 **Industrial Engineering Departments** for setting piece rates in wage incentive systems.
- 🤖 **Robotics & Ergonomics** for validating human-equivalent cycle times.
- 🪑 **Workplace Design** for workstation layout optimization.
- 📊 **Lean Manufacturing** to identify non-value-adding motions (waste elimination).
- 🏥 **Healthcare & Surgical Workflows** for procedure time benchmarking.
- 🏗️ **Construction** for time studies of repetitive activities (e.g., bricklaying, formwork).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Standard Six-Step MTM Procedure (KTU Lab Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme lab manual prescribes a **systematic six-step methodology** for every MTM-based standard time determination. Each step is documented in your lab record.

**Step 1 — Select the Job / Operation**
Choose a well-defined, repetitive manual task. Example: *Placing a bolt on a fixture and tightening it by hand*.

**Step 2 — Conduct Method Study (Motion Analysis)**
Break the operation into its **sequence of therbligs** (basic motions) by observing the operator. Use **memo-motion analysis** or **video recording** if the cycle time is too short for direct observation.

**Step 3 — Identify the Motion Type and Case**
For each therblig, consult the **MTM data tables** to find the matching motion type (R, M, T, G, P, RL, D, AP) and the appropriate sub-case (distance range, object size, fit type, etc.).

**Step 4 — Assign TMU Values**
Read the **TMU value** corresponding to each motion × sub-case combination from the standard MTM data card.

**Step 5 — Sum the TMU Values to Obtain Normal Time**
Add up all TMU values for one complete cycle. Convert to minutes by multiplying by **0.0006**.

**Step 6 — Apply Rating Factor and Add Allowances**
Multiply the sum by the rating factor (default = 1.0) to get **Normal Time (NT)**. Add the appropriate **allowance percentage** to arrive at the **Standard Time (ST)**.

---

### 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example — KTU Pattern

#### **Problem Statement**

> The operation of *picking up a cylindrical pin (2 cm diameter) lying on the workbench, moving it 40 cm, and inserting it into a circular hole* has been analyzed. The motion sequence, with the applicable MTM data, is given in the table below. The personal, fatigue, and contingency allowance is **15%**. The rating factor is taken as **1.0**. Calculate the **Standard Time** for the operation.

| Step | Motion | Description | Case | TMU Value |
|------|--------|-------------|------|-----------|
| 1 | **R** | Reach to pin (40 cm) | Case D | **16** |
| 2 | **G** | Grasp pin (small object) | G1A | **2** |
| 3 | **M** | Move pin to hole (40 cm) | Case D | **16** |
| 4 | **P** | Position pin into hole | P1SE | **15.4** |
| 5 | **RL** | Release pin | RL1 | **2** |

#### **Step-by-Step Exhaustive Solution**

**Step A — Sum the TMU Values for One Cycle**

$$\sum TMU = 16 + 2 + 16 + 15.4 + 2$$

$$\sum TMU = 51.4 \text{ TMU per cycle}$$

**Step B — Convert TMU to Minutes (Normal Time)**

$$NT = \left(\sum TMU\right) \times 0.0006 \text{ min/TMU}$$

$$NT = 51.4 \times 0.0006$$

$$NT = 0.03084 \text{ minutes per cycle}$$

**Step C — Apply the Rating Factor**

Since the problem states **R = 1.0**:

$$NT_{\text{adjusted}} = 0.03084 \times 1.0 = 0.03084 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step D — Compute the Allowance (15%)**

$$A = NT \times \dfrac{15}{100} = 0.03084 \times 0.15$$

$$A = 0.004626 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step E — Compute the Standard Time**

$$ST = NT + A = 0.03084 + 0.004626$$

$$\boxed{ST = 0.035466 \text{ minutes} \approx 2.128 \text{ seconds per cycle}}$$

**Step F — Convert to More Readable Units**

$$ST = 0.035466 \times 60 = 2.128 \text{ seconds per cycle}$$

$$ST = 0.035466 \times 1000 = 35.466 \text{ seconds per 1000 cycles}$$

---

### 3.3 Second Worked Example — With Multiple Sub-Cycles

> An MTM analysis of a simple **"Pick, Inspect, and Place"** operation yields the following motion data for **one inspection cycle** of a small component:

| Motion | Description | TMU |
|--------|-------------|-----|
| R-A | Reach for part (5 cm) | 2 |
| G-1A | Grasp small part | 2 |
| M-B | Move part to eye (25 cm) | 10 |
| ET | Eye Travel to gauge | 5 |
| EF | Eye Focus on dial | 7.3 |
| M-B | Move part to bin (25 cm) | 10 |
| RL1 | Release part | 2 |

If the operator performs **120 parts per hour (8-hour shift)** at a **rating of 100%**, and the allowance is **20%**, find the **Standard Time per part** and verify if the operator is meeting the standard.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sum TMU per Part**

$$\sum TMU = 2 + 2 + 10 + 5 + 7.3 + 10 + 2 = 38.3 \text{ TMU}$$

**Step 2 — Normal Time in Minutes**

$$NT = 38.3 \times 0.0006 = 0.02298 \text{ min/part}$$

**Step 3 — Standard Time per Part**

$$ST = NT \times \left(1 + \dfrac{20}{100}\right) = 0.02298 \times 1.2$$

$$\boxed{ST = 0.02758 \text{ min/part} \approx 1.655 \text{ seconds/part}}$$

**Step 4 — Standard Hourly Output**

$$\text{Standard output} = \dfrac{60 \text{ min}}{0.02758 \text{ min/part}} = 2175 \text{ parts per hour (theoretical)}$$

**Step 5 — Operator's Actual Performance**

$$Efficiency = \dfrac{120}{2175} \times 100 = 5.52\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The operator is working *far below* standard. The industrial engineer must investigate whether the MTM analysis missed some motions, or whether there is a **method problem** (training, tool design, layout, fatigue).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use MTM vs. Stopwatch Study

| Criterion | MTM (Predetermined) | Stopwatch Time Study |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Time to conduct** | Long (hours of analysis) | Short (few cycles) |
| **Skill required** | Trained MTM analyst | Trained observer |
| **Operator required** | **Not required** (synthetic) | **Required** for observation |
| **Accuracy** | High (lab-validated data) | Depends on observer skill |
| **Best for** | New jobs, short cycles, ergonomics | Existing jobs, long cycles |
| **Cost** | Higher (data tables, training) | Lower |
| **Effect on operator** | None (off-line) | Possible Hawthorne effect |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The MTM Standard Time Determination Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Define the Operation] --> stepB[Step 1: Conduct Method Study]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 2: Break Job into Basic Motions Therbligs]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 3: Classify Each Motion Type]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 4: Determine Distance and Case]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 5: Refer to MTM Data Table]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 6: Assign TMU Value]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 7: Sum All TMU Values]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 8: Convert TMU to Normal Time NT]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 9: Apply Rating Factor R]
    stepJ --> stepK[Step 10: Compute Allowance A]
    stepK --> stepL[Step 11: Calculate Standard Time ST]
    stepL --> stepM[Step 12: Document and Report]
    stepM --> endA[End: Standard Time Established]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — MTM Motion Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rootA[MTM Basic Motions] --> branchB[Reach R]
    rootA --> branchC[Move M]
    rootA --> branchD[Turn T]
    rootA --> branchE[Grasp G]
    rootA --> branchF[Position P]
    rootA --> branchG[Release RL]
    rootA --> branchH[Disengage D]
    rootA --> branchI[Apply Pressure AP]
    branchB --> caseB1[Case A 2 inch]
    branchB --> caseB2[Case B 12 inch]
    branchB --> caseB3[Case C 16 inch]
    branchB --> caseB4[Case D 32 inch]
    branchB --> caseB5[Case E above 32 inch]
    branchC --> caseC1[Case A]
    branchC --> caseC2[Case B]
    branchC --> caseC3[Case C]
    branchC --> caseC4[Case D]
    branchC --> caseC5[Case E]
    branchD --> caseD1[30 degrees]
    branchD --> caseD2[45 degrees]
    branchD --> caseD3[60 degrees]
    branchD --> caseD4[90 degrees]
    branchD --> caseD5[120 degrees]
    branchE --> caseE1[Very Small Object G1A]
    branchE --> caseE2[Small Object G1B]
    branchE --> caseE3[Large Object G1C]
    branchF --> caseF1[Symmetry Easy P1SE]
    branchF --> caseF2[Symmetry Difficult P1SD]
    branchF --> caseF3[No Symmetry P2SE]
    branchF --> caseF4[No Symmetry Difficult P2SD]
    branchH --> caseH1[Loose Fit D1]
    branchH --> caseH2[Light Fit D2]
    branchH --> caseH3[Medium Fit D3]
    branchH --> caseH4[Tight Fit D4]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — TMU Conversion Reference Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    tmuStart[Sum of TMU per Cycle] --> tmuToSec[Multiply by 0.036 to get Seconds]
    tmuStart --> tmuToMin[Multiply by 0.0006 to get Minutes]
    tmuStart --> tmuToHr[Multiply by 0.00001 to get Hours]
    tmuToMin --> ntStep[NT equals Sum TMU times Rating Factor]
    ntStep --> allowStep[Add Allowance NT times A percent]
    allowStep --> stFinal[ST equals Standard Time per Cycle]
    stFinal --> capacityStep[Capacity equals Available Time divided by ST]
    stFinal --> outputStep[Output equals 1 divided by ST in hours]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** These flowcharts serve as the **mental model** examiners expect students to draw on the answer sheet. For higher marks, **label the conversion constants** (0.0006, 0.036, 0.00001) directly on the arrows.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Methods Time Measurement (MTM). What is a TMU and what is its value in seconds?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Methods Time Measurement (MTM)** is a predetermined motion time system in which a manual task is broken down into its **basic human motions (therbligs)**, and the time for each motion is read from a **standard MTM data table** and summed to obtain the **normal time** of the task.
>
> **TMU (Time Measurement Unit)** is the fundamental unit of time used in the MTM system.
>
> **Value:** 1 TMU = 0.036 seconds = 0.0006 minutes = 0.00001 hours. (3 Marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any **six** basic motions recognized by the MTM system with their symbols.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The six basic motions of MTM are:
> 1. **Reach (R)** — Empty-hand movement to an object.
> 2. **Move (M)** — Hand movement carrying an object.
> 3. **Turn (T)** — Rotational motion of forearm or wrist.
> 4. **Grasp (G)** — Closing fingers around an object.
> 5. **Position (P)** — Aligning or engaging an object.
> 6. **Release (RL)** — Letting go of an object.
>
> (½ Mark each × 6 = 3 Marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **MTM procedure** for determining the standard time of a manual operation. State clearly the role of **TMU** and the **Rating Factor** in the calculation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An operator performs a *pick-and-place* operation. The MTM analysis yields the following motion data per cycle:

| Motion | TMU |
|--------|-----|
| R-D (Reach 45 cm) | 16 |
| G-1A (Grasp small) | 2 |
| M-D (Move 45 cm) | 16 |
| P-1SE (Position easy) | 5.6 |
| RL1 (Release) | 2 |

The **rating factor is 1.10** and the **allowance is 18%**. Calculate the **Normal Time**, the **Allowance**, and the **Standard Time** in **minutes** and **seconds** per cycle. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)**

The MTM procedure for determining standard time consists of the following steps:

**Step 1 — Select the Operation** *(1 Mark)*
Choose a clearly defined, repetitive manual task suitable for synthetic time study.

**Step 2 — Conduct Method Study** *(1 Mark)*
Observe the operator (or use video) and **break the operation into a sequence of basic human motions** (therbligs). This is called *motion analysis*.

**Step 3 — Identify Motion Type and Distance Case** *(1 Mark)*
For each motion, determine its **type** (R, M, T, G, P, RL, D, AP) and the **sub-case** based on distance, object size, or fit type.

**Step 4 — Assign TMU Values from Data Table** *(1 Mark)*
Read the **TMU value** corresponding to each motion-sub-case combination from the standard MTM data card. The TMU is a pre-fixed time value derived from extensive laboratory experiments on normal operators.

**Step 5 — Sum TMU and Compute Normal Time** *(1 Mark)*
$$\text{NT} = \left(\sum TMU\right) \times R \times 0.0006 \text{ min}$$

Here, the **Rating Factor (R)** adjusts the TMU-based normal time if the operator's actual performance deviates from the *normal-qualified* benchmark assumed in the MTM tables. If R = 1.0, no adjustment is made.

**Step 6 — Add Allowances** *(1 Mark)*
$$\text{ST} = \text{NT} + A = \text{NT} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{A\%}{100}\right)$$

Allowances cover **personal needs, fatigue, and contingencies**.

**Step 7 — Document the Result** *(1 Mark)*
Record the standard time, motion chart, and any recommendations for method improvement.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Sum of TMU Values** *(1 Mark)*

$$\sum TMU = 16 + 2 + 16 + 5.6 + 2 = 41.6 \text{ TMU/cycle}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Normal Time (with R = 1.10)** *(2 Marks)*

$$NT = \left(\sum TMU\right) \times 0.0006 \times R$$

$$NT = 41.6 \times 0.0006 \times 1.10$$

$$NT = 0.02496 \times 1.10 = 0.027456 \text{ minutes/cycle}$$

$$NT = 0.027456 \times 60 = 1.6474 \text{ seconds/cycle}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Allowance (18%)** *(2 Marks)*

$$A = NT \times \dfrac{18}{100} = 0.027456 \times 0.18$$

$$A = 0.004942 \text{ minutes} = 0.2965 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Standard Time** *(2 Marks)*

$$ST = NT + A = 0.027456 + 0.004942$$

$$\boxed{ST = 0.032398 \text{ minutes/cycle} \approx 1.944 \text{ seconds/cycle}}$$

| Quantity | Value in Minutes | Value in Seconds |
|----------|------------------|------------------|
| Normal Time | 0.027456 min | 1.6474 s |
| Allowance (18%) | 0.004942 min | 0.2965 s |
| **Standard Time** | **0.032398 min** | **1.944 s** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)** and **Stopwatch Time Study**. State **four advantages** of MTM over stopwatch studies. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In an MTM analysis of a hand-filing operation, the following motion data was recorded:

| Motion | TMU |
|--------|-----|
| R-C (Reach 35 cm) | 12 |
| G-1B (Grasp medium) | 3.5 |
| M-C (Move 35 cm) | 12 |
| T-90 (Turn 90°) | 9.1 |
| AP (Apply pressure) | 10.6 |
| RL1 (Release) | 2 |

If the rating factor is **1.0** and the total allowance is **25%**, find the **Standard Time per cycle in seconds** and the **number of cycles** possible in an **8-hour shift**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)**

**Comparison Table:** *(4 Marks)*

| Aspect | MTM (PMTS) | Stopwatch Time Study |
|--------|------------|----------------------|
| Basis | Synthetic — uses pre-determined data tables | Direct observation with stopwatch |
| Operator | Not required | Operator must be observed |
| Data source | Laboratory-generated motion time data | Field observation of the actual operator |
| Skill required | Trained MTM analyst | Trained work-study officer |
| Application | Suitable for short-cycle, repetitive jobs | Suitable for long-cycle jobs |
| Time consumption | Time-consuming to set up | Quick to conduct |
| Accuracy | High (consistent) | Variable (observer bias) |
| Rating factor | Default = 1.0 (built-in) | Must be applied (Westinghouse, etc.) |

**Four Advantages of MTM over Stopwatch Study:** *(3 Marks — ¾ each)*

1. **No operator is required** during analysis — MTM can determine standard time even before production starts.
2. **Consistent and unbiased** — eliminates observer errors and rating variability since the data is lab-validated.
3. **Useful for very short cycles** — stopwatches cannot reliably measure sub-5-second motions, but MTM handles them with ease.
4. **Suitable for method comparison** — alternative methods can be evaluated *on paper* without physically building prototypes.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Sum TMU Values** *(1 Mark)*

$$\sum TMU = 12 + 3.5 + 12 + 9.1 + 10.6 + 2 = 49.2 \text{ TMU/cycle}$$

**Step 2 — Normal Time in Minutes (R = 1.0)** *(2 Marks)*

$$NT = 49.2 \times 0.0006 \times 1.0 = 0.02952 \text{ min/cycle}$$

**Step 3 — Standard Time (Allowance = 25%)** *(2 Marks)*

$$ST = NT \times \left(1 + \dfrac{25}{100}\right) = 0.02952 \times 1.25$$

$$ST = 0.0369 \text{ min/cycle}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Seconds** *(1 Mark)*

$$ST = 0.0369 \times 60 = 2.214 \text{ seconds/cycle}$$

**Step 5 — Number of Cycles in 8-hour Shift (480 min)** *(1 Mark)*

$$N = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{ST \text{ per cycle}} = \dfrac{480}{0.0369}$$

$$\boxed{N = 13008 \text{ cycles per 8-hour shift}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks — Heed These!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the Rating Factor.** When the problem states R = 1.0 or R = 1.10, students often skip the multiplication entirely. **(–1 to –2 Marks)**
> 2. **Using the wrong conversion constant.** 1 TMU = 0.0006 min (for minutes) or 0.036 sec (for seconds). Mixing them up yields a 60× error. **(–2 to –3 Marks)**
> 3. **Adding allowance as an absolute value, not a percentage of NT.** Allowances are always computed as a *fraction of Normal Time*, not Standard Time. **(–1 Mark)**
> 4. **Omitting the units in the final answer.** Always state *minutes/cycle*, *seconds/piece*, or *parts/hour* explicitly.
> 5. **Not stating the assumption R = 1.0** explicitly in the solution. Examiners reward explicit assumptions. **(–½ Mark)**
> 6. **Missing the "Position" sub-case distinction.** P-1SE and P-1SD have very different TMU values; do not assume a single value for all Position motions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam**

- ⭐ **MTM** = *Methods Time Measurement* — a **predetermined motion time system** developed by **Maynard, Stegemerten & Schwab (1948)**.
- ⭐ **TMU** = *Time Measurement Unit* — **1 TMU = 0.036 s = 0.0006 min = 0.00001 h**.
- ⭐ **Eight Basic Motions:** Reach (R), Move (M), Turn (T), Grasp (G), Position (P), Release (RL), Disengage (D), Apply Pressure (AP).
- ⭐ **Five Distance Cases (Reach/Move):** A (≤2"), B (2–12"), C (12–16"), D (16–32"), E (>32").
- ⭐ **Core Formula:** $\sum TMU \xrightarrow{\times 0.0006} NT \xrightarrow{\times R} NT_{adj} \xrightarrow{+A} ST$
- ⭐ **Allowance Formula:** $ST = NT \times \left(1 + \dfrac{A\%}{100}\right)$
- ⭐ **Default Rating Factor R = 1.0** for MTM (TMU data already assumes normal operator).
- ⭐ **MTM is SYNTHETIC** — no stopwatch, no operator observation needed.
- ⭐ **Best for:** Short, repetitive, manual cycles (< 1 minute).
- ⭐ **Allowance components:** Personal needs, Fatigue, Contingencies (typical 15% – 25%).
- ⭐ **Capacity Formula:** $N = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{ST \text{ per cycle}}$
- ⭐ **Remember the MTM "Recipe Analogy"** — the data table is a *recipe book*, the therbligs are *cooking steps*, TMU is the *time per step*, and ST is the *total cook time*.
- ⭐ **Always label units** — minutes/cycle, seconds/piece, or pieces/hour.
- ⭐ **Common Lab Manual Motions:** R-D, M-D, P-1SE, RL1, G-1A, AP, T-90 are the most frequently tested.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of principles of motion economy in the workplace
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIEL408 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Application of principles of motion economy in the workplace # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Principles of Motion Economy** are a codified set of work-design guidelines, originally formulated by **Frank Gilbreth** and later expanded by industrial engineering scholars, that govern the efficient use of **human body movements**, **workplace layout**, and **tool/equipment design** to minimize human energy expenditure, reduce motion fatigue, and maximize operator productivity during the performance of a manual task. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Note:** In the Work System Design Lab (PCIEL408), these principles are treated as the *third layer* of method improvement — applied **after** the operation process chart and flow process chart have established the macro-workflow, and **alongside** the *therbligs* (basic elemental motions) micro-analysis. ### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Motion Economy Motion economy principles are universally classified into exactly three categories in the KTU 2024 lab manual: | Pillar # | Category Name | Primary Focus | |:---:|:---|:---| | **Pillar I** | Principles Related to the Human Body | Use of hands, arms, and body parts | | **Pillar II** | Principles Related to the Workplace | Layout, illumination, bench height | | **Pillar III** | Principles Related to Equipment & Tool Design | Jigs, fixtures, controls, materials handling | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Chef" Intuition 🍳 Imagine a professional chef preparing a dish in a kitchen that has been **deliberately mis-arranged**: - Knives placed on the **far-right** shelf while the cutting board is on the **far-left** (long arm reach every cut). - Spices stored on the **floor** instead of at chest level (constant bending). - Only **one hand** used for everything, even when stirring and pouring could be done simultaneously. - No hooks for ladles, so the chef **grips** them continuously between uses. Now imagine the **same chef** in a *motion-economized* kitchen: - Knives mounted on a **magnetic strip at elbow height** directly above the cutting board. - Spices on a **rotating carousel** at chest level. - Both hands used **symmetrically and simultaneously** for chopping. - Ladles **hung from a ceiling rack** — released and recovered without gripping. > [!NOTE] > **The Economy of Motion** is exactly this: *eliminating the wasteful movements a worker makes within an already-optimized process flow.* The chef still cooks the same dish — but burns **fewer calories per plate** and produces **more plates per hour**. ### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics Used in Motion Economy Studies When applying these principles in PCIEL408 lab experiments, the standard engineering metrics tracked are: - **Normal Time (TN)** — measured in **seconds (s)** or **minutes (min)** - **Basic Time (TB)** — the unrelaxed, standard-paced time - **Rating Factor (R)** — dimensionless, typically **100 ± 15** - **Standard Time (TS)** — in **seconds per cycle** - **Number of Motions (Therbligs)** — dimensionless count - **Cycle Time Reduction (%)** — dimensionless percentage > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight:** A "motion-economized workplace" in KTU 2024 evaluation expects you to demonstrate at least **one principle from each of the three pillars** during your viva-voce. Memorize this 3-pillar structure — it is the most heavily tested concept. # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Gilbreth Principles — Exhaustive Master List ### 🟦 PILLAR I — Principles Related to the Human Body (7 Principles) These principles govern *how the operator uses their own anatomy*. They address hand usage, symmetry, simultaneity, rhythm, and fatigue distribution. | # | Principle (Verbatim) | Engineering Justification | |:---:|:---|:---| | **H1** | Both hands should *begin* and *end* their motions at the same time. | Synchronization eliminates one hand's idle waiting time. | | **H2** | Both hands should *not be idle* at the same time, except for rest periods. | Idle time is the cardinal sin of motion study. | | **H3** | Motions of the arms should be *simultaneous, symmetrical, and opposite*. | Bilateral symmetry uses muscle groups efficiently. | | **H4** | Hand motions should be confined to the *lowest practical classification* (finger > wrist > elbow > shoulder). | Lower limb joints consume less energy per unit motion. | | **H5** | *Rhythm* and *uniformity* of motion should be maintained. | Predictable rhythm reduces cognitive load and micro-pauses. | | **H6** | The number of basic motions (therbligs) should be *minimized*. | Fewer elementary motions = shorter cycle time. | | **H7** | *Eye travel* should be minimized; the workplace should permit attention without shifting gaze. | Visual hunting adds 0.3–0.5 s per shift. | ### 🟩 PILLAR II — Principles Related to the Workplace (5 Principles) These principles address the *static geometry* of the workstation — where things sit in 3D space relative to the seated or standing operator. | # | Principle (Verbatim) | Engineering Justification | |:---:|:---|:---| | **W1** | There should be a *definite and fixed place* for all tools and materials. | Eliminates search motions (each search = ~2 s lost). | | **W2** | Tools and materials should be located to permit the *best sequence* of motions. | Aligns the layout with the therblig sequence. | | **W3** | *Gravity* should be utilized wherever possible (drop deliveries, ejectors). | Replaces muscular effort with gravitational potential energy. | | **W4** | Adequate *illumination, ventilation, and temperature* must be provided. | Comfort directly affects the *rating factor (R)*. | | **W5** | Bench height should permit *alternating sitting and standing*. | Reduces static muscular fatigue of the lower back. | ### 🟧 PILLAR III — Principles Related to Equipment & Tool Design (5 Principles) These principles govern the *design of the instruments* the operator interacts with. | # | Principle (Verbatim) | Engineering Justification | |:---:|:---|:---| | **E1** | *Fingers* and hands should be relieved of work that the *feet* can do. | Distributes effort across muscle groups (e.g., foot pedals). | | **E2** | Two or more tools should be *combined* wherever possible. | Reduces tool-change motions and therblig count. | | **E3** | Tools and materials should be *pre-positioned* at the point of use. | Eliminates reach + grasp + position sequence. | | **E4** | *Drop deliveries* and *ejectors* should be used to remove finished work. | Replaces place/position motions with gravity releases. | | **E5** | Controls, displays, and fasteners should be *located close to the operator*. | Shrinks the operator's *normal working area*. | ## 2.2 The "Normal Working Area" Concept — Spatial Geometry The operator's reachable space is divided into three concentric zones, originally defined by **Frederick Winslow Taylor** and refined by the Gilbreths: - **Normal Working Area (NWA):** The arc swept by both hands with elbows resting at the sides — radius ≈ **25–30 cm**. - **Maximum Working Area (MWA):** The arc reached when arms are extended forward — radius ≈ **50 cm**. - **Vertical Working Plane:** A surface inclined **15°–30°** toward the operator, allowing objects to slide toward them by gravity. > [!NOTE] > **The 17 motion economy principles are sometimes counted as 16 or 22 depending on the textbook edition.** KTU 2024 follows the **17-principle classification** (7+5+5) shown above, which is the most current standard used in the lab manual. ## 2.3 The Motion Economy Analysis Workflow — Standard 5-Stage Procedure This is the **mandatory procedure** you must follow during the PCIEL408 lab record submission: 1. **Record** the existing method using a film/video or direct observation. 2. **Analyze** the motion sequence using *therbligs* (basic elemental motions). 3. **Apply** the 17 motion-economy principles one-by-one. 4. **Synthesize** an *improved method* incorporating all valid suggestions. 5. **Evaluate** the new method's cycle time and compare with the original. ## 2.4 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Symbol Description | Units | |:---|:---|:---| | $$T_{N} = T_{O} \times R$$ | Normal Time = Observed Time × Rating Factor | **seconds (s)** | | $$T_{S} = T_{N} + \text{Allowances}$$ | Standard Time = Normal Time + Relaxation Allowance | **seconds (s)** | | $$\% \text{Reduction} = \frac{T_{\text{old}} - T_{\text{new}}}{T_{\text{old}}} \times 100$$ | Percentage Cycle Time Reduction | **dimensionless (%)** | | $$\text{Number of Therbligs reduced} = n_{\text{old}} - n_{\text{new}}$$ | Reduction in Basic Motions | **dimensionless** | | $$\text{Motion Economy Index} = \frac{n_{\text{effective}}}{n_{\text{total}}}$$ | Ratio of Useful Motions to Total | **dimensionless (0–1)** | | $$T_{\text{saved per cycle}} = T_{S,\text{old}} - T_{S,\text{new}}$$ | Time Saved per Cycle | **seconds (s)** | | $$T_{\text{saved per shift}} = T_{\text{saved per cycle}} \times \frac{28800}{T_{\text{cycle,new}}}$$ | Time Saved per 8-h Shift (28800 s) | **seconds (s)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Conditions for Validity:** > - Rating factor $R$ is bounded: **85 ≤ R ≤ 115**. > - Relaxation allowance typically: **10%–20%** of Normal Time. > - The percentage reduction formula assumes $T_{\text{old}} \neq 0$. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - 🏭 **Manufacturing Assembly Lines:** Used to design automotive assembly stations (e.g., Toyota Production System). - 🏥 **Surgical Ergonomics:** Laparoscopic instrument layout in operating theatres. - 💻 **UX/UI Design:** Applying motion economy to *digital* cursor travel and screen real-estate. - 📦 **Warehousing:** Picker-station layout design using "slotting to order frequency." - ⌨️ **Keyboard & Tool Design:** QWERTY layout, screwdriver handle grip engineering. --- # 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Application Procedure & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Full Lab Experiment Application — Step-by-Step Walkthrough Below is the **complete worked procedure** that a PCIEL408 student must document in the lab record. We will apply all 17 principles to a generic *"Bolt-and-Washer Assembly"* task and quantify the savings. ### 🔢 GIVEN DATA (Assumed for Lab Analysis) - Original operation: Manually assembling **1 bolt + 1 washer + 1 nut** onto a fixture. - Original cycle time observed: $T_O = 48.0$ s. - Operator rating: $R = 105$ (slightly above standard pace). - Original therblig count: $n_{\text{old}} = 23$ therbligs. - Allowance applied: $A = 15\%$. ### 📐 STEP 1 — Compute Original Standard Time $$ \begin{aligned} T_{N,\text{old}} &= T_O \times R \\ &= 48.0 \times \frac{105}{100} \\ &= 50.4 \text{ s} \end{aligned} $$ Now the standard time: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{S,\text{old}} &= T_{N,\text{old}} \times (1 + A) \\ &= 50.4 \times 1.15 \\ &= 57.96 \text{ s per cycle} \end{aligned} $$ > The original method therefore produces: > $$\text{Output}_{\text{old}} = \frac{28800 \text{ s}}{57.96 \text{ s/cycle}} \approx 496.9 \text{ pieces per 8-h shift.}$$ --- ### 📐 STEP 2 — Apply Pillar I Principles (Human Body) | Original Action | New Action | Principle Applied | |:---|:---|:---| | Left hand holds bolt; right hand reaches for washer. | Both hands pick up bolt and washer **simultaneously** from symmetrical bins. | H1, H2, H3 | | Reaching for nut from shoulder height. | Nut pre-positioned at **finger level** on the fixture. | H4 | | Operator looks back and forth between bolt and washer. | Bin layout aligned so **eye travel is zero**. | H7 | **Result:** Reduced therbligs from 23 to **17**. --- ### 📐 STEP 3 — Apply Pillar II Principles (Workplace) | Original Action | New Action | Principle Applied | |:---|:---|:---| | Tools scattered on the bench. | Each tool on a **shadow-board** with a fixed slot. | W1 | | Heavy toolbox at foot level. | Toolbox at **elbow height** on a tilt rack. | W2 | | Operator manually places the finished bolt into a bin. | Finished part **slides down a chute** to a collection bin. | W3 | | Workbench too low, forcing stooping. | Adjustable bench allows **sit-stand alternation**. | W5 | **Result:** Saved approximately **5.4 seconds** from reach and search motions. --- ### 📐 STEP 4 — Apply Pillar III Principles (Equipment & Tool Design) | Original Action | New Action | Principle Applied | |:---|:---|:---| | Foot pedal used for clamping; hand used for tightening. | **Foot pedal** continues clamping; **hand ratchet** tightens (no change here, validates E1). | E1 | | Separate wrench and screwdriver used. | **Combination tool** (ratchet + bit-holder) used. | E2 | | Operator manually loads the bolt onto a fixture. | **Gravity-fed vibratory bowl feeder** pre-positions bolts. | E3, E4 | **Result:** Saved another **3.6 seconds**. --- ### 📐 STEP 5 — Compute New Standard Time Total time saved = 5.4 s + 3.6 s = **9.0 s**. $$ \begin{aligned} T_{O,\text{new}} &= 48.0 - 9.0 = 39.0 \text{ s} \\ T_{N,\text{new}} &= 39.0 \times \frac{105}{100} = 40.95 \text{ s} \\ T_{S,\text{new}} &= 40.95 \times 1.15 = 47.09 \text{ s per cycle} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### 📐 STEP 6 — Compute Improvement Metrics $$ \begin{aligned} \% \text{ Cycle Reduction} &= \frac{57.96 - 47.09}{57.96} \times 100 \\ &= 18.76\% \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Therblig Reduction} &= 23 - 17 = 6 \text{ therbligs} \\ \text{Motion Economy Index}_{\text{new}} &= \frac{17 - \text{waste therbligs}}{17} = \frac{14}{17} \approx 0.82 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{New Output} &= \frac{28800}{47.09} \approx 611.6 \text{ pieces per shift} \\ \text{Additional Output per Shift} &= 611.6 - 496.9 = \mathbf{114.7 \text{ extra pieces}} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.2 The Motion Economy Analytical Matrix (KTU Lab Record Format) This 6-column matrix is the **standard reporting format** expected by KTU examiners in the lab record: | S.No | Original Step | Therblig(s) | Principle(s) Violated | Improved Step | New Therblig(s) | Time Saved (s) | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---:| | 1 | Reach for washer | R, G | H2, H4 | Pre-position at finger level | R (short) | 1.2 | | 2 | Search for wrench | S, R | W1 | Shadow-board slot | — | 1.8 | | 3 | Lift finished part to bin | M, R, P, RL | W3 | Drop chute | RL only | 2.4 | | 4 | Two-handed asymmetric assembly | A1, A2 | H1, H3 | Symmetric simultaneous | A1 + A2 sync | 1.8 | | 5 | Eye travel between bins | — | H7 | Bin alignment | — | 1.8 | | | | | | | **TOTAL** | **9.0 s** | --- ## 3.3 Why Each Principle Works — The "Why" Behind the "How" - **Symmetry (H3)** works because the human body is bilaterally symmetrical; asymmetric loading creates lateral muscle imbalance and fatigue. - **Pre-positioning (E3)** works because each therblig removed eliminates 2–4 additional dependent therbligs (no reach → no grasp → no move → no position). - **Gravity utilization (W3, E4)** works because gravity provides **9.81 m/s²** of acceleration — free of metabolic cost to the operator. - **Sit-stand alternation (W5)** works because static standing loads the lumbar discs at **~140%** of seated load. --- # 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Three Pillars — Concept Map ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[PRINCIPLES OF MOTION ECONOMY]:::root --> nodeB[PILLAR I
Human Body]:::pillar1 nodeA --> nodeC[PILLAR II
Workplace]:::pillar2 nodeA --> nodeD[PILLAR III
Equipment and Tools]:::pillar3 nodeB --> nodeB1[Both hands symmetric]:::leaf nodeB --> nodeB2[Lowest limb class]:::leaf nodeB --> nodeB3[Minimize eye travel]:::leaf nodeB --> nodeB4[Minimize therbligs]:::leaf nodeC --> nodeC1[Fixed tool locations]:::leaf nodeC --> nodeC2[Use gravity]:::leaf nodeC --> nodeC3[Comfortable environment]:::leaf nodeC --> nodeC4[Sit stand flexibility]:::leaf nodeD --> nodeD1[Combine tools]:::leaf nodeD --> nodeD2[Pre-position materials]:::leaf nodeD --> nodeD3[Use drop ejectors]:::leaf nodeD --> nodeD4[Foot for foot work]:::leaf classDef root fill:#1f2937,color:#fff,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:3px classDef pillar1 fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff,stroke:#1d4ed8 classDef pillar2 fill:#10b981,color:#fff,stroke:#047857 classDef pillar3 fill:#f97316,color:#fff,stroke:#c2410c classDef leaf fill:#f3f4f6,color:#000,stroke:#9ca3af ``` ## 4.2 The 5-Stage Application Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR stepA[Stage 1
RECORD method
via video or memo]:::stage --> stepB[Stage 2
ANALYZE
using therbligs]:::stage stepB --> stepC[Stage 3
APPLY
17 principles]:::stage stepC --> stepD[Stage 4
SYNTHESIZE
improved method]:::stage stepD --> stepE[Stage 5
EVALUATE
compare cycle times]:::stage stepE --> stepF[OUTPUT
Reduced TS and
higher output]:::result classDef stage fill:#0ea5e9,color:#fff,stroke:#0369a1 classDef result fill:#22c55e,color:#fff,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 4.3 The Normal / Maximum Working Area Geometry ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph VIEW[TOP DOWN VIEW OF OPERATOR] direction TB nodeO[OPERATOR
TORSO CENTER]:::op --> nodeNA[NORMAL AREA
both hands
elbows at side]:::zone1 nodeO --> nodeMA[MAXIMUM AREA
