M1 Hand Area] K --> L[Spinal Cord
Corticospinal Tract] L --> M[Hand Muscles
Button Press
Motor Execution] H -.T detection 50 to 70 ms.-> I I -.T selection variable.-> J J -.T execution 80 to 100 ms.-> M ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagram:** Stages `H → I → J` constitute the **central (cognitive) processing** that varies between tasks. Stages `A → B → C → D → E → F → G → H` and `K → L → M` are **peripheral** and remain approximately constant. ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Experimental Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Lab Session Start]) --> prep[Subject Briefing
and Informed Consent] prep --> demo[Calibrate Headphones
to 70 dB SPL] demo --> warm[10 Practice Trials
Per Task Discarded] warm --> t1[Task 1 Simple RT
30 Trials Recorded] t1 --> rest1[3 Minute Rest] rest1 --> t2[Task 2 Choice RT
30 Trials 2 Way] t2 --> rest2[3 Minute Rest] rest2 --> t3[Task 3 Discrimination
30 Trials] t3 --> rest3[3 Minute Rest] rest3 --> t4[Task 4 Go or No Go
Optional 30 Trials] t4 --> compute[Compute Mean and
Standard Deviation
and CV] compute --> stats[Paired t Test Across
Task Conditions] stats --> report[Lab Report with
Discussion and
Hick Law Verification] report --> end([Lab Session End]) compute --> err[Error Analysis
False Starts
Wrong Responses] err --> report ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Hick's Law Visualization ```mermaid flowchart LR n1[1 Choice
Simple RT
Approx 150 ms] --> n2[2 Choices
Approx 230 ms
Plus 80 ms] n2 --> n4[4 Choices
Approx 310 ms
Plus 80 ms] n4 --> n8[8 Choices
Approx 390 ms
Plus 80 ms] n8 --> nx[n Choices
RT equals a plus
b log2 n] ``` > The graph demonstrates Hick's Law: doubling the number of choices adds a **fixed time penalty** (≈ **80–100 ms**) corresponding to the cortical decision cost per bit of information. ## 4.4 Apparatus Interconnection Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR PC[Stimulus Computer
Runs PsychoPy or
E Prime Software] -->|Audio Out
3.5 mm Jack| HP[Headphones
70 dB SPL] PC -->|USB Trigger| RB[Response Box
2 Microswitches
or Keyboard] RB -->|USB HID| PC PC -->|Logs CSV| LOG[(Data File
Trial RT ms
Error Flag)] SLM[Sound Level Meter
Calibration Only] -.->|Daily Check| HP ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Define auditory reaction time. Mention any two factors that affect it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Auditory reaction time is the time interval between the onset of an auditory stimulus and the initiation of a pre-defined motor response by the subject, typically measured in milliseconds. **(1 mark)** Factors affecting it: 1. **Stimulus intensity:** Higher loudness levels (up to ~100 dB) reduce RT; very high levels cause startle and increase RT. 2. **Task complexity:** Choice and discrimination tasks add 80–150 ms over simple RT. 3. **Subject's age and arousal level, fatigue, practice, caffeine, and stimulus frequency** are also key factors. **(2 marks)** ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **State Hick's Law. Why is it relevant to auditory alarm design in a control room?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Hick's Law (1952):** $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n)$, where $n$ is the number of equally probable stimulus-response alternatives. **(2 marks)** **Relevance:** In a control room, an operator may be confronted with multiple simultaneous alarms. By *minimizing* the number of distinct alarm sounds and ensuring stimulus-response compatibility (e.g., one sound = one pre-learned action), the designer keeps $n$ small and thus keeps $RT$ low, leaving more time for corrective action. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** *`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`* **(a)** Describe, with a neat block diagram, the experimental setup and procedure to determine the auditory reaction time of a subject for (i) simple RT, (ii) 2-choice RT, and (iii) discrimination RT. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A subject performs 5 trials of a simple auditory reaction time task with the following readings (in ms): 162, 178, 155, 190, 165. Compute the mean, standard deviation, and coefficient of variation. Comment on the consistency of the subject's responses. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution** **(a) Setup and Procedure — 7 marks** **Apparatus (2 marks):** - Auditory stimulus generator (PC + software such as PsychoPy / E-Prime, or a dedicated hardware tone generator). - Closed-back stereo headphones, calibrated to **70 dB SPL** at the ear. - Two-button response box (or QWERTY keyboard with designated keys). - Sound level meter, stopwatch, recording proforma. **Procedure (4 marks):** 1. The subject is seated comfortably, briefed, and consent is taken. Headphones are fitted; volume is calibrated to 70 dB. 2. **Task 1 — Simple RT:** A single **1000 Hz, 100 ms tone** is presented. Subject presses the response key with the dominant index finger as fast as possible. Inter-stimulus interval randomized 2–5 s. **30 trials** recorded after 10 practice trials. 3. **Task 2 — 2-Choice RT:** Two tones (500 Hz and 2000 Hz) are presented in pseudo-random order. Subject presses LEFT for low pitch and RIGHT for high pitch. **30 trials** (15 each). 4. **Task 3 — Discrimination RT:** A stream of 1000 Hz tones contains occasional 1500 Hz target tones. Subject responds only to targets. **30 trials.** 5. Errors, false starts (RT < 100 ms), and lapses (RT > 1000 ms) are flagged. 6. A 3-minute rest is given between tasks. **Block Diagram (1 mark):** *Refer to the Mermaid apparatus interconnection diagram in Section 4.4.* **(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks** **Data:** 162, 178, 155, 190, 165 ms; $n = 5$. **Step 1: Mean.** $$\overline{RT} = \frac{162 + 178 + 155 + 190 + 165}{5} = \frac{850}{5} = 170 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ **Step 2: Deviations and squared deviations.** | $RT_i$ | $RT_i - 170$ | $(RT_i - 170)^2$ | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 162 | $-8$ | $64$ | | 178 | $+8$ | $64$ | | 155 | $-15$ | $225$ | | 190 | $+20$ | $400$ | | 165 | $-5$ | $25$ | | **Sum** | — | **$778$** | **Step 3: Standard deviation (sample, $n-1$ denominator).** $$s = \sqrt{\frac{778}{5 - 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{778}{4}} = \sqrt{194.5} \approx 13.95 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ **Step 4: Coefficient of variation.** $$CV = \frac{13.95}{170} \times 100\% \approx 8.21\% \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ **Comment (2 marks):** The mean RT of 170 ms lies within the expected range (140–170 ms) for simple auditory RT. The CV of 8.21% is well below 15%, indicating the subject gave **consistent, low-variance** responses. The data is reliable and the subject was attentive throughout the trials. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** *`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`* **(a)** With the help of Hick's Law, explain why choice reaction time is always greater than simple reaction time. How would you use this law in designing an auditory warning system for a nuclear power plant control room? **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a 2-choice auditory reaction time experiment, a subject produced the following RT values (in ms) for the LOW tone: 245, 260, 238, 255, 248, 270, 242. For the HIGH tone the values are: 252, 268, 244, 261, 255, 278, 249. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether there is a significant difference between the RTs for the two tones. (Use paired t-test, $t_{crit,\,6,\,0.05} = 2.447$.) **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution** **(a) Hick's Law and Warning System Design — 7 marks** **Hick's Law derivation (3 marks):** As the number of equiprobable stimulus-response alternatives $n$ increases, the information content of the stimulus (in bits) increases as $H = \log_2(n)$ bits. The organism needs to process this information before responding, so: $$RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n)$$ For $n = 1$ (simple RT), $\log_2(1) = 0$, so $RT = a$ (the minimum, ≈ 150 ms for auditory stimuli). For $n = 2$ (2-choice RT), $\log_2(2) = 1$, so $RT = a + b$ (≈ 230 ms). Thus, choice RT is always $\geq$ simple RT, with the difference equal to $b$ per binary decision stage. **Application to nuclear control room (4 marks):** 1. **Minimize the number of distinct alarm sounds** so that each alarm has a one-to-one mapping with a known corrective action. Keep $n$ small. 2. **Use stimulus-response compatibility:** Pitch can encode *urgency* (rising pitch → higher urgency) and *location* (stereo left/right ear → side of plant). This reduces cognitive search time. 3. **Group alarms hierarchically:** Master alarm + sub-alarm pattern, rather than 50 unique sounds. This compresses the effective $n$. 4. **Conduct regular drills** so that operators' effective $b$ coefficient (per-bit processing time) decreases with practice, lowering total RT. 5. **Ensure total operator RT (detection + decision + actuation) is < 1 s** for any critical alarm, per IAEA safety guideline NS-G-1.3. **(b) Paired t-Test Solution — 7 marks** **Data pairing** (Subject = same, so each pair is a trial index): | Pair | Low RT (X) | High RT (Y) | $d = Y - X$ | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 1 | 245 | 252 | $+7$ | | 2 | 260 | 268 | $+8$ | | 3 | 238 | 244 | $+6$ | | 4 | 255 | 261 | $+6$ | | 5 | 248 | 255 | $+7$ | | 6 | 270 | 278 | $+8$ | | 7 | 242 | 249 | $+7$ | **Step 1: Mean difference. (2 marks)** $$\overline{d} = \frac{7 + 8 + 6 + 6 + 7 + 8 + 7}{7} = \frac{49}{7} = 7.0 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 2: Standard deviation of differences. (2 marks)** $$\sum (d_i - 7)^2 = 0^2 + 1^2 + (-1)^2 + (-1)^2 + 0^2 + 1^2 + 0^2 = 4$$ $$s_d = \sqrt{\frac{4}{7 - 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{4}{6}} = \sqrt{0.6667} \approx 0.8165 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 3: t-statistic. (1 mark)** $$t = \frac{\overline{d}}{s_d / \sqrt{n}} = \frac{7.0}{0.8165 / \sqrt{7}} = \frac{7.0}{0.8165 / 2.6458} = \frac{7.0}{0.3086} \approx 22.68$$ **Step 4: Comparison and conclusion. (2 marks)** Since $t_{calc} = 22.68 \gg t_{crit,\,6,\,0.05} = 2.447$, we **reject the null hypothesis** $H_0: \mu_d = 0$ at the 5% significance level. **Conclusion:** There is a **statistically significant difference** between the RTs for the LOW and HIGH tones. The HIGH tone RT is consistently larger (by ~7 ms on average). Possible reasons: high-frequency tones (2000 Hz) may be slightly less comfortable at 70 dB, or the subject may have a marginal sensory asymmetry, or pitch-recognition latency is slightly higher for higher frequencies. In a work design context, the engineer should standardize alarm pitches to a frequency band (typically 500–2000 Hz) where ART is minimized. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in this experiment — KTU 2024 Examiner Feedback:** > 1. **Forgetting to randomize the Inter-Stimulus Interval (ISI).** If ISI is constant (e.g., always 2 s), the subject will *anticipate* and false-start, biasing RT downward. **Always use 2–5 s randomized ISI.** (–2 marks) > 2. **Including practice-trial data in the final analysis.** Practice trials are explicitly to be discarded. (–1 mark) > 3. **Using $n$ instead of $n-1$ in the standard deviation formula** (population vs. sample). For a lab of 5–10 subjects, always use $n-1$. (–1 mark) > 4. **Failing to flag false starts (RT < 100 ms) and lapses (RT > 1000 ms).** These are physiological outliers, not measurement errors. (–1 mark) > 5. **Not reporting the sound pressure level (dB SPL).** The examiner will deduct marks if you do not state that the stimulus was calibrated (e.g., **70 dB SPL at the ear**). (–1 mark) > 6. **Omitting the "Discussion" section in the lab record.** KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) rubric allocates 2–3 marks to the *interpretation* of results, not just the calculations. (–2 marks) > 7. **Mis-stating Hick's Law** as linear in $n$ instead of logarithmic. (–2 marks) --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Auditory Reaction Time (ART):** Time (in **ms**) from onset of auditory stimulus to motor response initiation. Typical simple ART ≈ **140–170 ms**. - **Three stages of RT** (Donders): *Stimulus detection* (≈ 50–70 ms, fixed) + *Response selection* (0–150 ms, task-dependent) + *Motor execution* (≈ 80–100 ms, fixed). - **Task hierarchy** in the lab: Simple (1 stimulus, 1 response) → Choice (n stimuli, n responses) → Discrimination (target + distractors) → Go/No-Go (response inhibition). - **Hick's Law:** $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n)$. Doubling $n$ adds a constant penalty $b$ (≈ 80–100 ms) per binary decision. - **Always calibrate** stimulus to **70 dB SPL** at the ear; document this in the apparatus section. - **Randomize ISI** between **2 and 5 seconds** to prevent anticipatory responses. - **Conduct 10 practice trials** per task, then **30 recorded trials** per task. - **Compute mean $\overline{RT}$, sample standard deviation $s$ (using $n-1$), and coefficient of variation $CV$.** - **Expected CV** for reliable data is **< 15%**; if higher, suspect fatigue or distraction → discard and retest. - **Flag and exclude** RT < 100 ms (anticipation) and RT > 1000 ms (lapse). - **Use paired t-test** when comparing two tasks on the same subjects: $t = \dfrac{\overline{d}}{s_d / \sqrt{n}}$. - **Engineering applications:** alarm/horn design, control-room safety, assembly-line pacing, ATC voice protocols, prosthetic auditory feedback. - **Viva-favorite questions:** (i) Why is auditory RT faster than visual RT? (ii) State and explain Hick's Law. (iii) What is the role of the ISI? (iv) Differentiate between simple, choice, and discrimination RT. - **Formulae to memorize cold:** Mean, sample SD, CV%, Hick's Law, paired t-test, Channel capacity $V_c = d / RT$. - **OBE mapping (KTU 2024):** This experiment primarily satisfies **CO2** ("*Conduct experiments to evaluate human-factor parameters in work systems*") and **CO4** ("*Analyze and interpret experimental data using statistical tools*").

topics.title: Determination of energy expenditure for different activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIEL408 - WORK SYSTEM DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of energy expenditure for different activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Energy Expenditure (EE)** is the total amount of metabolic energy, expressed in **kilocalories (kcal)** or **kilojoules (kJ)**, that a human body consumes to sustain life processes and perform physical work over a given period. In Work System Design and Industrial Ergonomics, energy expenditure is the principal physiological metric used to **classify workloads, design job-rest schedules, determine manning levels, and establish fair wage incentives (e.g., Halsey, Rowan, Bedeaux plans).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> *Energy expenditure is the rate at which a worker's body expends energy during the performance of a given task, measured indirectly through oxygen consumption, heart rate, or directly through calorimetry, and is the fundamental input to Physical Work Capacity (PWC) analysis.*

### Component Hierarchy of Total Energy Expenditure

$$
EE_{Total} = EE_{BMR} + EE_{Specific Dynamic Action} + EE_{Physical Activity} + EE_{Thermoregulation}
$$

| Component | Typical Contribution | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR)** | **60–70 %** | Energy for vital organ function at complete rest |
| **Physical Activity (Work)** | **20–30 %** | Energy for muscular work performed |
| **Specific Dynamic Action (SDA)** | **~10 %** | Energy cost of digesting food |
| **Thermoregulation** | **<5 %** | Maintaining body temperature at 37 °C |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Engine" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the human body exactly like the engine of a car.
> - **BMR ≈ Idling Fuel Consumption:** Even when parked, the engine burns fuel to keep the radiator circulating, battery charged, and oil pumping. Similarly, the body burns energy for the heart, brain, and lungs, even when you are "doing nothing."
> - **Physical Activity ≈ Driving on a Highway:** The harder you accelerate (lift weights, climb stairs), the more fuel is injected per minute.
> - **Oxygen (O₂) ≈ Intake Air:** The engine measures air to inject fuel; physiologists measure O₂ to calculate the "fuel" (energy) burned.
> - **Douglas Bag ≈ Exhaust Gas Analyser:** Captures the "exhaust" (expired air) to deduce how much air-fuel mixture was consumed.

This is why **1 litre of oxygen consumed ≈ 5 kcal of energy produced** (a universal constant for a mixed diet). It is the body's "air-to-fuel" conversion factor.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Units (Board-Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these constants — they appear in every KTU numerical:**
> - **1 L O₂ consumed ≈ 5.0 kcal ≈ 20.9 kJ**
> - **1 MET (Metabolic Equivalent) = 3.5 mL O₂ / kg body weight / min**
> - **1 kcal = 4.186 kJ**
> - **RQ (Respiratory Quotient) for mixed diet = 0.85**
> - **STP (Standard Temperature & Pressure): 0 °C, 1 atm, Dry**
> - **Body Surface Area (Du Bois): BSA (m²) = 0.007184 × W⁰·⁴²⁵ × H⁰·⁷²⁵**

---

## 1.4 Categories of Workload (KTU Board Classification Standard)

| Category | EE (kcal/min) | VO₂ (L/min) | Heart Rate (bpm) | Example Task |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Light Work** | < 5.0 | < 0.5 | < 90 | Desk work, inspection, typing |
| **Moderate Work** | 5.0 – 7.5 | 0.5 – 1.0 | 90 – 110 | Walking on level ground, light assembly |
| **Heavy Work** | 7.5 – 10.0 | 1.0 – 1.5 | 110 – 130 | Shoveling, fast walking, cycle pedaling |
| **Very Heavy Work** | 10.0 – 12.5 | 1.5 – 2.0 | 130 – 150 | Climbing stairs with load, mining |
| **Extremely Heavy Work** | > 12.5 | > 2.0 | > 150 | Maximal sprint, heavy construction |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** The classification is based on **average energy expenditure per minute** for a standardized 70 kg reference worker. Any deviation in body mass must be normalized using the **Metabolic Equivalent of Task (MET)** scale.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Three Pillars of EE Measurement

### Pillar 1: Direct Calorimetry
The most accurate but impractical method. The subject is sealed inside a chamber (Atwater-Benedict calorimeter) and the heat dissipated by the body is measured directly using thermocouples. Used in research, not in industrial labs.

### Pillar 2: Indirect Calorimetry (Industrial Standard)
Since **all biological energy production is oxidative**, measuring the volume of oxygen consumed is equivalent to measuring energy burned. The human body does not store oxygen, so **VO₂ is a real-time proxy for energy expenditure.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The First Law of Thermodynamics applied to humans:**
> *Energy released by oxidation of food = Energy as mechanical work + Energy as heat. Since oxidation requires O₂ and produces CO₂, we can track it through respiration.*

### Pillar 3: Heart Rate (Pulse) Method
Because cardiac output (and thus heart rate) is linearly correlated with oxygen consumption above a certain threshold, HR is used as a non-invasive field estimator. The **resting HR + 40 bpm** rule is widely used in KTU problems.

## 2.2 The Operational Logic Chain (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

**Step 1 → Baseline Establishment**
- Subject rests in supine position for **30 minutes** in thermoneutral environment (22–25 °C).
- Measure baseline VO₂ and HR. This gives **BMR/RMR**.

**Step 2 → Activity Performance**
- Subject performs the standardized task (walking, lifting, cycling) for a **fixed duration (5–10 min)** to reach **steady state** (HR stabilizes within ±5 bpm).

**Step 3 → Expired Air Collection**
- Breathe through a low-resistance valve. Expired air goes into a **Douglas Bag** (200 L capacity, leak-proof, two-way valve).
- Duration of collection is timed precisely with a stopwatch.

**Step 4 → Volume Measurement**
- Pass the collected air through a **wet gas meter** or **dry gas meter** to get **V_E (expired ventilation) in L/min STPD**.

**Step 5 → Gas Analysis**
- Analyze O₂ and CO₂ fractions using a **Haldane apparatus**, **paramagnetic O₂ analyzer**, or **infrared CO₂ analyzer**.
- Compare with inspired air: F_IO₂ = 20.93 %, F_ICO₂ = 0.03 %.

**Step 6 → Calculation of VO₂**

$$
VO_2 = V_E \times \left( F_{IO_2} - F_{EO_2} \right) \quad \text{[Simplified, neglects RQ correction]}
$$

For more accuracy (Haldane transformation):

$$
VO_2 = V_E \times \left[ \frac{(1 - F_{EO_2} - F_{ECO_2}) \times F_{IO_2}}{1 - F_{IO_2}} - F_{EO_2} \right]
$$

**Step 7 → Conversion to Energy**

$$
EE \; (\text{kcal/min}) = VO_2 \; (L/min) \times 5.0 \; \text{kcal/L}
$$

**Step 8 → Classification & Reporting**
Compute **PAR (Physical Activity Ratio)**:

$$
PAR = \frac{EE_{\text{activity}}}{EE_{\text{resting/BMR}}}
$$

Use PAR × 24 hours to estimate **Total Daily Energy Expenditure** for wage/incentive design.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board Examination Ready)

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $EE = VO_2 \times 5.0$ | Oxygen-to-energy conversion | kcal/min | Universal |
| 2 | $EE = VO_2 \times 20.9$ | Oxygen-to-energy conversion | kJ/min | SI problems |
| 3 | $VO_2 = V_E (F_{IO_2} - F_{EO_2})$ | VO₂ without Haldane correction | L/min | Quick field calc |
| 4 | $VO_2 = V_E \times \left[\frac{(1-F_{EO_2}-F_{ECO_2}) F_{IO_2}}{1-F_{IO_2}} - F_{EO_2}\right]$ | Accurate Haldane formula | L/min | Board exam |
| 5 | $RQ = \frac{V_{CO_2}}{V_{O_2}}$ | Respiratory Quotient | dimensionless | Diet classification |
| 6 | $BMR_{men} = 66.47 + 13.75W + 5.00H - 6.76A$ | Harris-Benedict (Males) | kcal/day | Resting EE |
| 7 | $BMR_{women} = 655.10 + 9.56W + 1.85H - 4.68A$ | Harris-Benedict (Females) | kcal/day | Resting EE |
| 8 | $PAR = \frac{EE_{work}}{EE_{rest}}$ | Physical Activity Ratio | dimensionless | Workload class |
| 9 | $TDEE = BMR \times 1.1 + \sum (EE_i \times t_i)$ | Total Daily EE | kcal/day | Job design |
| 10 | $BSA = 0.007184 \cdot W^{0.425} \cdot H^{0.725}$ | Du Bois Body Surface Area | m² | Heat stress |
| 11 | $1 \; MET = 3.5 \; mL O_2 / kg / min$ | Metabolic Equivalent | mL/kg/min | Standard tasks |
| 12 | $EE_{net} = (HR_{work} - HR_{rest}) \times 0.014 \times W$ | Heart-rate linear approx | kcal/min | Field estimate |

> Where: W = weight (kg), H = height (cm), A = age (years), t_i = duration of activity i (min).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Work-Study & Method Study:** Energy cost identifies wasteful motions and helps in motion-economy redesign.
> - **Wage Incentive Design:** The **Halsey Premium Plan** and **Rowan Plan** both use the time saved to compute bonus; an over-fatiguing job requires shorter work cycles to prevent injury.
> - **Ergonomic Job Rotation:** Workers are rotated between light, moderate, and heavy tasks so that 8-hour average EE stays below **5 kcal/min** (ILO recommended limit).
> - **Manpower Planning:** Industrial Engineering uses **Work Physiological standards** (e.g., **Kogi, Edholm, or Christensen scales**) to set permissible work cycles.
> - **Rehabilitation & Sports Science:** VO₂ max testing (Bruce Protocol, Astrand-Rhyming test) directly descends from this lab principle.

---

## 2.5 Comparative Study of Measurement Methods

| Method | Equipment | Accuracy | Cost | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Douglas Bag Method** | Douglas bag, gas meter, Haldane analyzer | High (±2 %) | Low | **High** ⭐ |
| **Kofranyi-Michaelis Meter** | Portable dry gas meter + sampling bag | Moderate (±5 %) | Moderate | **High** ⭐ |
| **Oxylog (Portable)** | Electronic O₂/CO₂ sensor | High (±1 %) | High | Medium |
| **Heart-Rate Monitor** | Polar chest strap / smartwatch | Low-Moderate (±10 %) | Very Low | Medium |
| **Direct Calorimetry** | Atwater-Benedict chamber | Very High | Very High | Low (theory only) |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Expenditure from Douglas Bag Data (Board-Standard)

### Problem Setup
A worker performs a lifting task. The expired air collected in a Douglas bag for **6 minutes** has a total volume of **300 litres at STPD**. Analysis shows expired air contains **16.80 % O₂** and **3.80 % CO₂**. Atmospheric (inspired) air contains **20.93 % O₂** and **0.03 % CO₂**. The worker's body mass is **75 kg**. Classify the workload.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate Expired Ventilation Rate (V_E)**

$$
V_E = \frac{\text{Total Volume Collected}}{\text{Duration}} = \frac{300 \; L}{6 \; min}
$$

$$
V_E = 50 \; L/min
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Gas Fractions**

$$
F_{IO_2} = 0.2093, \quad F_{ICO_2} = 0.0003
$$

$$
F_{EO_2} = 0.1680, \quad F_{ECO_2} = 0.0380
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Haldane Transformation (Accurate VO₂)**

$$
VO_2 = V_E \times \left[ \frac{(1 - F_{EO_2} - F_{ECO_2}) \times F_{IO_2}}{1 - F_{IO_2}} - F_{EO_2} \right]
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
VO_2 = 50 \times \left[ \frac{(1 - 0.1680 - 0.0380) \times 0.2093}{1 - 0.2093} - 0.1680 \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify Inside Brackets**

$$
1 - 0.1680 - 0.0380 = 0.7940
$$

$$
\frac{0.7940 \times 0.2093}{1 - 0.2093} = \frac{0.166182}{0.7907} = 0.21017
$$

$$
0.21017 - 0.1680 = 0.04217
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by V_E**

$$
VO_2 = 50 \times 0.04217 = 2.1085 \; L/min
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Energy Expenditure**

$$
EE = VO_2 \times 5.0 = 2.1085 \times 5.0
$$

$$
\boxed{EE = 10.54 \; kcal/min}
$$

**Step 7 — Workload Classification**

Since 10.54 kcal/min lies in the **7.5 – 10.0 kcal/min** band? No, it is just above — actually **10.0 – 12.5 kcal/min** → this falls under **VERY HEAVY WORK**.

Wait — re-checking: 10.54 > 10.0, so it is **VERY HEAVY WORK** (board examiner loves this verification step).

**Step 8 — Express in kJ**

$$
EE = 10.54 \times 4.186 = 44.12 \; kJ/min
$$

**Step 9 — Calculate PAR (assuming BMR of 75 kg male = 1.20 kcal/min)**

$$
PAR = \frac{EE_{work}}{BMR} = \frac{10.54}{1.20} = 8.78
$$

A PAR > 7 is indicative of exhausting labor — this task exceeds sustainable 8-hour limits.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: BMR Estimation Using Harris-Benedict Equation

### Problem Setup
A **28-year-old male worker**, weighing **65 kg** and **172 cm tall**, is to be evaluated. Compute his **BMR (kcal/day)** and the **total daily energy expenditure** if he performs moderate work (EE = 5.5 kcal/min) for **8 hours** and rests (EE = 1.25 kcal/min) for the remaining **16 hours**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply Harris-Benedict Equation for Males**

$$
BMR = 66.47 + 13.75 W + 5.00 H - 6.76 A
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values**

$$
BMR = 66.47 + 13.75 \times 65 + 5.00 \times 172 - 6.76 \times 28
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Each Term**

$$
13.75 \times 65 = 893.75
$$

$$
5.00 \times 172 = 860.00
$$

$$
6.76 \times 28 = 189.28
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the Terms**

$$
BMR = 66.47 + 893.75 + 860.00 - 189.28
$$

$$
BMR = 66.47 + 893.75 = 960.22
$$

$$
960.22 + 860.00 = 1820.22
$$

$$
1820.22 - 189.28 = 1630.94 \; kcal/day
$$

$$
\boxed{BMR \approx 1631 \; kcal/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Per-Minute BMR for PAR Calculation**

$$
BMR_{min} = \frac{1630.94}{24 \times 60} = 1.132 \; kcal/min
$$

**Step 6 — Energy During 8-Hour Work**

$$
EE_{work} = 5.5 \times 8 \times 60 = 2640 \; kcal
$$

**Step 7 — Energy During 16-Hour Rest**

$$
EE_{rest} = 1.25 \times 16 \times 60 = 1200 \; kcal
$$

**Step 8 — Total Daily Energy Expenditure (TDEE)**

$$
TDEE = 2640 + 1200 = 3840 \; kcal/day
$$

$$
\boxed{TDEE = 3840 \; kcal/day}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Check:** TDEE is roughly 2.35 × BMR, which is consistent with a "moderately active" lifestyle (1.4 to 2.4 × BMR per FAO/WHO standards). This validates our answer.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heart-Rate Based Field Estimation

### Problem Setup
A worker's resting heart rate is **72 bpm** and working heart rate (steady state) is **128 bpm**. His body mass is **70 kg**. Estimate energy expenditure using the linear HR-VO₂ method.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Heart Rate Increment**

$$
\Delta HR = HR_{work} - HR_{rest} = 128 - 72 = 56 \; bpm
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Linear Approximation Formula**

$$
EE_{net} = \Delta HR \times 0.014 \times W
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**

$$
EE_{net} = 56 \times 0.014 \times 70
$$

$$
EE_{net} = 56 \times 0.98
$$

$$
EE_{net} = 54.88 \; kcal/hr = 0.915 \; kcal/min
$$

**Step 4 — Add BMR Contribution**

$$
EE_{total} = EE_{net} + BMR_{min}
$$

For 70 kg male, BMR/min ≈ 1.20 kcal/min:

$$
EE_{total} = 0.915 + 1.20 = 2.115 \; kcal/min
$$

$$
\boxed{EE \approx 2.12 \; kcal/min}
$$

**Step 5 — Classification**
EE = 2.12 kcal/min → **Light Work** (less than 5 kcal/min). This task is suitable for 8-hour continuous work without physiological strain.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting a Method

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Controlled laboratory, high accuracy needed | **Douglas Bag + Haldane** | Gold standard, ±2 % error |
| Field study on construction site | **Kofranyi-Michaelis meter** | Portable, rugged |
| Continuous monitoring of a nurse/call-center worker | **HR Monitor (Polar)** | Unobtrusive, cheap |
| Heat stress evaluation in a foundry | **Indirect calorimetry + HR** | Combine EE with HR for safety index |
| Designing wage incentive for a factory | **Douglas Bag (typical worker sample)** | Establishes PAR baseline |
| Rehabilitation assessment of injured worker | **Oxylog (electronic)** | Real-time VO₂ max tracking |
| Large population survey (n > 100) | **HR method** | Practical, statistical averaging |

---

## 3.5 KTU 2024 Common Numerical Trap — Pre-calculated Reference

| Activity | VO₂ (L/min, 70 kg) | EE (kcal/min) | HR (bpm) | MET | PAR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reclining | 0.20 | 1.00 | 65 | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| Sitting, reading | 0.25 | 1.25 | 70 | 1.3 | 1.2 |
| Standing, relaxed | 0.30 | 1.50 | 75 | 1.5 | 1.4 |
| Walking 3 km/h | 0.55 | 2.75 | 95 | 2.5 | 2.5 |

| Heavy manual digging | 2.50 | 12.50 | 175 | 10.0 | 11.4 |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Energy Expenditure Measurement Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the complete, end-to-end process of determining energy expenditure in the KTU PCIEL408 lab, mapping every functional block, decision gate, and analytical stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STAGE 1: Subject Preparation] --> A1[30 min supine rest, thermoneutral room 22 to 25 C]
    A1 --> A2[Record Age, Weight, Height, Sex of subject]
    A2 --> A3[Measure Resting HR and Resting VO2 baseline]
    A3 --> B[STAGE 2: Activity Performance]
    B --> B1[Subject performs standardized task for 5 to 10 min]
    B1 --> B2[Wait for steady state: HR within plus minus 5 bpm]
    B2 --> C[STAGE 3: Expired Air Collection]
    C --> C1[Two way breathing valve directs expired air to Douglas Bag]
    C1 --> C2[Note exact collection time t in minutes]
    C2 --> D[STAGE 4: Volume Measurement]
    D --> D1[Pass collected air through calibrated wet gas meter]
    D1 --> D2[Read VE in L per min STPD]
    D2 --> E[STAGE 5: Gas Composition Analysis]
    E --> E1[Analyze FEO2 using paramagnetic O2 sensor]
    E --> E2[Analyze FECO2 using infrared CO2 sensor]
    E2 --> F[STAGE 6: VO2 Calculation]
    F --> F1[Apply Haldane equation or simplified formula]
    F1 --> F2[Compute VO2 in L per min]
    F2 --> G[STAGE 7: Energy Conversion]
    G --> G1[EE kcal per min equals VO2 times 5.0]
    G --> G2[EE kJ per min equals VO2 times 20.9]
    G2 --> H[STAGE 8: Classification and Reporting]
    H --> H1[Compute PAR equals EE work divided by BMR]
    H --> H2[Classify as Light, Moderate, Heavy, or Very Heavy]
    H2 --> I[STAGE 9: Interpretation]
    I --> I1[Compare with ILO permissible limits]
    I --> I2[Recommend job rotation or rest schedule]
    I2 --> J[FINAL OUTPUT: TDEE and Workload Category]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each rectangular block represents a **functional stage** in the lab procedure. The arrows (→) show the **sequence of operations**; decision-like blocks (steady-state check) are explicit gates the examiner expects you to mention verbally.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Indirect Calorimetry Hardware Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INSP[Inspiration Side]
        I1[Atmospheric Air 20.93 percent O2] --> I2[One way Inlet Valve]
    end
    subgraph BODY[Subject]
        S[Lungs and Muscles performing task]
    end
    subgraph EXSP[Expiration Side]
        I2 --> S
        S --> E1[One way Outlet Valve]
        E1 --> E2[Douglas Bag 200 L capacity]
        E2 --> E3[Wet Gas Meter]
        E3 --> E4[Haldane Gas Analyzer]
        E4 --> E5[Data: VE, FEO2, FECO2]
    end
    E5 --> CALC[Calculator: VO2 and EE]
    CALC --> CLASS[Classify Workload]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Practical Tip:** During the KTU viva, always mention the **two-way non-rebreathing valve** (e.g., Hans Rudolph valve) — it ensures **inspired and expired air streams never mix**, which is critical for accurate VO₂ measurement.

---

## 4.3 Process Flow — Heart Rate Method (Calibration & Field Use)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Calibration Step: Subject on Treadmill or Cycle] --> P2[Record HR and VO2 at 3 to 4 steady states]
    P2 --> P3[Plot HR on X axis, VO2 on Y axis]
    P3 --> P4[Fit linear regression: VO2 equals a plus b times HR]
    P4 --> P5[Obtain calibration constants a and b]
    P5 --> Q1[Field Use: Worker performs actual job]
    Q1 --> Q2[Record steady state HR using chest strap]
    Q2 --> Q3[Predict VO2 using calibration equation]
    Q3 --> Q4[Convert VO2 to EE by multiplying with 5.0]
    Q4 --> Q5[Output: Continuous EE in real time]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The HR method requires **individual calibration** because the slope **b** varies with fitness, age, and cardiovascular health. A "universal" HR-VO₂ line is inaccurate for KTU precision problems.
